{{Infobox Hieroglyphen |NAME = D1:D36 M44-X1 N14-N33 |NAME-TRANSKRIPTION = Tepi-a Sopdet
''Tpj-ˁ Spd.t''
''Forerunner of Sopdet'' |BILD1 = Position Beta Cma.png |BILD1-BREITE = 250px |BILD1-BESCHREIBUNG = Constellation “Sopdet” ([[Canis Major]]) }} '''Tepi-a Sopdet''' is the designation of an {{ill|Ancient Egypt|de|Altes Ägypten}} {{ill|Decade (Ancient Egypt)|de|Dekade (Altägypten)|1=Decan}}, which comprised only one {{ill|Baktiu|de|Baktiu}} (decan star). == Background == This decan corresponds to {{ill|Murzim|de|Murzim}}, the forerunner of {{ill|Sirius|de|Sirius}}. The traditional {{ill|Arabic language|de|Arabische Sprache}} name, "Harbinger," likely refers to its position, as it rises before Sirius and heralds the brightest star in the night sky. However, this role does not apply to {{ill|Heliacal rising|de|Heliakisch}} because Murzim is significantly dimmer than Sirius and requires a {{ill|Altitude|de|Sehungsbogen}} of at least 13° to become heliacally visible, or approximately 55 minutes before the sun rises. Furthermore, Murzim requires a minimum horizon altitude of 1.7° due to atmospheric refraction. The same conditions apply to {{ill|Acronychal setting|de|Akronychisch}}. The entries in the {{ill|Book of Nut|de|Nutbuch}} regarding decan positions can therefore only refer to the acronychal {{ill|Culmination (astronomy)|de|Kulmination (Astronomie)}}, as only in this context could Murzim rank before Sirius in the decan lists. During the era of the {{ill|Diagonal star clock|de|Diagonalsternuhren}}, Murzim reached a culmination altitude of approximately 36.6° in Ancient Egypt. Sirius itself was part of the Ancient Egyptian constellation {{ill|Sopdet (constellation)|de|Sopdet (Sternbild)}}. {{ill|Alexandra von Lieven|fr|Alexandra von Lieven|de|Alexandra von Lieven}} notes the decan star "Tepi-a Sopdet" mentioned in the {{ill|Book of Nut|de|Nutbuch}}, which in the seventh year of {{ill|Senusret III|de|Sesostris III.}} was listed as the 34th decan with the {{ill|Heliacal rising|de|Heliakisch}} assigned to the {{ill|Peret#Dating|lt=6th Peret IV|de|6. Peret IV}} (approximately June 15/21); the {{ill|Culmination (astronomy)|de|Kulmination (Astronomie)}} for the {{ill|Achet#Dating|lt=26th Achet II|de|26. Achet II}}, and the acronychal setting for the 26th Peret I. The recorded period of invisibility lasted the canonical 70 days.Alexandra von Lieven: ''Grundriss des Laufes der Sterne''. p. 66. == Dating of Tepi-a Sopdet (Book of Nut System) == {| class="wikitable" style="width: 98%; border: 2px solid gray;" |- {{Ägyptologie TblHighlight}} |colspan="7" align=center style="background:#FFE39B"|'''Dating of Tepi-a Sopdet in Memphis''' |- {{Ägyptologie TblHighlight}} !Year ![[Culmination (astronomy)|Acronychal
Culmination]] ![[Acronychal|Acronychal
Setting]] ![[Heliacal rising|Heliacal
Rising]]The altitude values refer to the end of the respective night hour. Heliacal rising occurs before sunrise, acronychal setting, and acronychal culmination after sunset. !Remarks |- bgcolor="#fffaf0" | style="text-align:center" | 1873 BCE | style="text-align:center" | {{ill|Achet#Dating|lt=26th Achet II|de|26. Achet II}}
January 11–12
Altitude 30.3°Actual culmination: 36.5°, approximately 2.75 hours after sunset. | style="text-align:center" | {{ill|Peret#Dating|lt=26th Peret I|de|26. Peret I}}
April 11–12
Altitude 10.4°The observation of the setting was only possible towards the end of the decade. | style="text-align:center" | {{ill|Peret#Dating|lt=6th Peret IV|de|6. Peret IV}}
June 20–21
Altitude 2.3°On the 10th decade day, the altitude was 9.3°. A heliacal observation during the decade was therefore not possible. | style="text-align:center" | {{ill|Senusret III|de|Sesostris III.}} |- bgcolor="#f5f5f5" | style="text-align:center" | 1541 BCE | style="text-align:center" | 19th Peret I
January 14–15 | style="text-align:center" | 19th Peret IV
April 14–15 | style="text-align:center" | {{ill|Shemu#Dating|lt=29th Shemu II|de|29. Schemu II}}
June 23–24 | style="text-align:center" | {{ill|Ebers calendar|de|Ebers-Kalender}} |- bgcolor="#fffaf0" | style="text-align:center" | 1463 BCE | style="text-align:center" | 8th Peret II
January 15–16 | style="text-align:center" | 8th Shemu I
April 15–16 | style="text-align:center" | 18th Shemu III
June 24–25 | style="text-align:center" | {{ill|Ramesside star clocks|de|Ramessidische Sternuhren}} |- |} == Literature == * {{Ill|Christian Leitz|fr|Christian Leitz|en|Christian Leitz|de|Christian Leitz}}: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren''. Peeters, Leuven 1995, ISBN 90-6831-669-9, p. 95. * {{ill|Alexandra von Lieven|fr|Alexandra von Lieven|de|Alexandra von Lieven}}: ''Grundriss des Laufes der Sterne – Das sogenannte Nutbuch''. The Carsten Niebuhr Institute of Ancient Eastern Studies (et al.), Copenhagen 2007, ISBN 978-87-635-0406-5, pp. 62 and 398. == References == [[Category:Decan star (Ancient Egypt)]] [[Category:Ancient Egyptian Day]][[Category:Ancient Egyptian Day link incomplete]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:Tepi-a Sopdet]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:Tepi-a Sopdet]][[en:draft:Tepi-a Sopdet]] [[de:Tepi-a Sopdet]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:Tepi-a Sopdet]][[Category:Untranslated pages]] [[Category:Tepi-a Sopdet]][[Category:Ancient Egyptian Day]][[Category:Ancient Egyptian Day link incomplete]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:Tepi-a Sopdet]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:Tepi-a Sopdet]][[Category:bump 1-25]] [[Category:pgoodbye $1]][[Category:new pages]][[Category:Tepi-a Sopdet]][[Category:pgoodbye $1]][[Category:new pages]] [[Category:pgoodbye $1]][[Category:new pages]][[Category:Tepi-a Sopdet]][[Category:pgoodbye $1]][[Category:new pages]] [[sn:Tepi-a-Sopdet]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Tepi-a-Sopdet]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Tepi-a-Sopdet]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Tepi-a-Sopdet]][[Category:qq]]

revision.text:
{{Infobox Hieroglyphen |NAME = D1:D36 M44-X1 N14-N33 |NAME-TRANSKRIPTION = Tepi-a Sopdet
''Tpj-ˁ Spd.t''
''Forerunner of Sopdet'' |BILD1 = Position Beta Cma.png |BILD1-BREITE = 250px |BILD1-BESCHREIBUNG = Constellation “Sopdet” ([[Canis Major]]) }} '''Tepi-a Sopdet''' is the designation of an {{ill|Ancient Egypt|de|Altes Ägypten}} {{ill|Decade (Ancient Egypt)|de|Dekade (Altägypten)|1=Decan}}, which comprised only one {{ill|Baktiu|de|Baktiu}} (decan star). == Background == This decan corresponds to {{ill|Murzim|de|Murzim}}, the forerunner of {{ill|Sirius|de|Sirius}}. The traditional {{ill|Arabic language|de|Arabische Sprache}} name, "Harbinger," likely refers to its position, as it rises before Sirius and heralds the brightest star in the night sky. However, this role does not apply to {{ill|Heliacal rising|de|Heliakisch}} because Murzim is significantly dimmer than Sirius and requires a {{ill|Altitude|de|Sehungsbogen}} of at least 13° to become heliacally visible, or approximately 55 minutes before the sun rises. Furthermore, Murzim requires a minimum horizon altitude of 1.7° due to atmospheric refraction. The same conditions apply to {{ill|Acronychal setting|de|Akronychisch}}. The entries in the {{ill|Book of Nut|de|Nutbuch}} regarding decan positions can therefore only refer to the acronychal {{ill|Culmination (astronomy)|de|Kulmination (Astronomie)}}, as only in this context could Murzim rank before Sirius in the decan lists. During the era of the {{ill|Diagonal star clock|de|Diagonalsternuhren}}, Murzim reached a culmination altitude of approximately 36.6° in Ancient Egypt. Sirius itself was part of the Ancient Egyptian constellation {{ill|Sopdet (constellation)|de|Sopdet (Sternbild)}}. {{ill|Alexandra von Lieven|fr|Alexandra von Lieven|de|Alexandra von Lieven}} notes the decan star "Tepi-a Sopdet" mentioned in the {{ill|Book of Nut|de|Nutbuch}}, which in the seventh year of {{ill|Senusret III|de|Sesostris III.}} was listed as the 34th decan with the {{ill|Heliacal rising|de|Heliakisch}} assigned to the {{ill|Peret#Dating|lt=6th Peret IV|de|6. Peret IV}} (approximately June 15/21); the {{ill|Culmination (astronomy)|de|Kulmination (Astronomie)}} for the {{ill|Achet#Dating|lt=26th Achet II|de|26. Achet II}}, and the acronychal setting for the 26th Peret I. The recorded period of invisibility lasted the canonical 70 days.Alexandra von Lieven: ''Grundriss des Laufes der Sterne''. p. 66. == Dating of Tepi-a Sopdet (Book of Nut System) == {| class="wikitable" style="width: 98%; border: 2px solid gray;" |- {{Ägyptologie TblHighlight}} |colspan="7" align=center style="background:#FFE39B"|'''Dating of Tepi-a Sopdet in Memphis''' |- {{Ägyptologie TblHighlight}} !Year ![[Culmination (astronomy)|Acronychal
Culmination]] ![[Acronychal|Acronychal
Setting]] ![[Heliacal rising|Heliacal
Rising]]The altitude values refer to the end of the respective night hour. Heliacal rising occurs before sunrise, acronychal setting, and acronychal culmination after sunset. !Remarks |- bgcolor="#fffaf0" | style="text-align:center" | 1873 BCE | style="text-align:center" | {{ill|Achet#Dating|lt=26th Achet II|de|26. Achet II}}
January 11–12
Altitude 30.3°Actual culmination: 36.5°, approximately 2.75 hours after sunset. | style="text-align:center" | {{ill|Peret#Dating|lt=26th Peret I|de|26. Peret I}}
April 11–12
Altitude 10.4°The observation of the setting was only possible towards the end of the decade. | style="text-align:center" | {{ill|Peret#Dating|lt=6th Peret IV|de|6. Peret IV}}
June 20–21
Altitude 2.3°On the 10th decade day, the altitude was 9.3°. A heliacal observation during the decade was therefore not possible. | style="text-align:center" | {{ill|Senusret III|de|Sesostris III.}} |- bgcolor="#f5f5f5" | style="text-align:center" | 1541 BCE | style="text-align:center" | 19th Peret I
January 14–15 | style="text-align:center" | 19th Peret IV
April 14–15 | style="text-align:center" | {{ill|Season of the Harvest|lt=Shemu#Dating|lt=29th Shemu II|de|29. Schemu II}}
June 23–24 | style="text-align:center" | {{ill|Ebers calendar|de|Ebers-Kalender}} |- bgcolor="#fffaf0" | style="text-align:center" | 1463 BCE | style="text-align:center" | 8th Peret II
January 15–16 | style="text-align:center" | 8th Shemu I
April 15–16 | style="text-align:center" | 18th Shemu III
June 24–25 | style="text-align:center" | {{ill|Ramesside star clocks|de|Ramessidische Sternuhren}} |- |} == Literature == * {{Ill|Christian Leitz|fr|Christian Leitz|en|Christian Leitz|de|Christian Leitz}}: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren''. Peeters, Leuven 1995, ISBN 90-6831-669-9, p. 95. * {{ill|Alexandra von Lieven|fr|Alexandra von Lieven|de|Alexandra von Lieven}}: ''Grundriss des Laufes der Sterne – Das sogenannte Nutbuch''. The Carsten Niebuhr Institute of Ancient Eastern Studies (et al.), Copenhagen 2007, ISBN 978-87-635-0406-5, pp. 62 and 398. == References == [[Category:Decan star (Ancient Egypt)]] [[Category:Ancient Egyptian Day]][[Category:Ancient Egyptian Day link incomplete]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:Tepi-a Sopdet]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:Tepi-a Sopdet]][[en:draft:Tepi-a Sopdet]] [[de:Tepi-a Sopdet]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:Tepi-a Sopdet]][[Category:Ugoodbye $1 pages]] [[Category:Tepi-a Sopdet]][[Category:Ancient Egyptian Day]][[Category:Ancient Egyptian Day link incomplete]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:Tepi-a Sopdet]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:Tepi-a Sopdet]][[Category:bump 1-25]] [[Category:new pages]][[Category:Tepi-a Sopdet]][[Category:new pages]] [[Category:new pages]][[Category:Tepi-a Sopdet]][[Category:new pages]] [[sn:Tepi-a-Sopdet]] [[sn:Tepi-a-Sopdet]][[sn:Tepi-a-Sopdet]][[sn:Tepi-a-Sopdet]]

June 24–25 | style="text-align:center" | {{ill|Ramesside star clocks|de|Ramessidische Sternuhren}} |- |} == Literature == * {{Ill|Christian Leitz|fr|Christian Leitz|en|Christian Leitz|de|Christian Leitz}}: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren''. revision.comment: Adding [[Category:qq]][[sn:{{subst:PAGENAME}}]][[Category:qq]] using [[c:Help:Cat-a-lot|Cat-a-lot]]
revision.text:
{{Infobox Hieroglyphen |NAME = D1:D36 M44-X1 N14-N33 |NAME-TRANSKRIPTION = Tepi-a Sopdet
''Tpj-ˁ Spd.t''
''Forerunner of Sopdet'' |BILD1 = Position Beta Cma.png |BILD1-BREITE = 250px |BILD1-BESCHREIBUNG = Constellation “Sopdet” ([[Canis Major]]) }} '''Tepi-a Sopdet''' is the designation of an {{ill|Ancient Egypt|de|Altes Ägypten}} {{ill|Decade (Ancient Egypt)|de|Dekade (Altägypten)|1=Decan}}, which comprised only one {{ill|Baktiu|de|Baktiu}} (decan star). == Background == This decan corresponds to {{ill|Murzim|de|Murzim}}, the forerunner of {{ill|Sirius|de|Sirius}}. The traditional {{ill|Arabic language|de|Arabische Sprache}} name, "Harbinger," likely refers to its position, as it rises before Sirius and heralds the brightest star in the night sky. However, this role does not apply to {{ill|Heliacal rising|de|Heliakisch}} because Murzim is significantly dimmer than Sirius and requires a {{ill|Altitude|de|Sehungsbogen}} of at least 13° to become heliacally visible, or approximately 55 minutes before the sun rises. Furthermore, Murzim requires a minimum horizon altitude of 1.7° due to atmospheric refraction. The same conditions apply to {{ill|Acronychal setting|de|Akronychisch}}. The entries in the {{ill|Book of Nut|de|Nutbuch}} regarding decan positions can therefore only refer to the acronychal {{ill|Culmination (astronomy)|de|Kulmination (Astronomie)}}, as only in this context could Murzim rank before Sirius in the decan lists. During the era of the {{ill|Diagonal star clock|de|Diagonalsternuhren}}, Murzim reached a culmination altitude of approximately 36.6° in Ancient Egypt. Sirius itself was part of the Ancient Egyptian constellation {{ill|Sopdet (constellation)|de|Sopdet (Sternbild)}}. {{ill|Alexandra von Lieven|fr|Alexandra von Lieven|de|Alexandra von Lieven}} notes the decan star "Tepi-a Sopdet" mentioned in the {{ill|Book of Nut|de|Nutbuch}}, which in the seventh year of {{ill|Senusret III|de|Sesostris III.}} was listed as the 34th decan with the {{ill|Heliacal rising|de|Heliakisch}} assigned to the {{ill|Peret#Dating|lt=6th Peret IV|de|6. Peret IV}} (approximately June 15/21); the {{ill|Culmination (astronomy)|de|Kulmination (Astronomie)}} for the {{ill|Achet#Dating|lt=26th Achet II|de|26. Achet II}}, and the acronychal setting for the 26th Peret I. The recorded period of invisibility lasted the canonical 70 days.Alexandra von Lieven: ''Grundriss des Laufes der Sterne''. p. 66. == Dating of Tepi-a Sopdet (Book of Nut System) == {| class="wikitable" style="width: 98%; border: 2px solid gray;" |- {{Ägyptologie TblHighlight}} |colspan="7" align=center style="background:#FFE39B"|'''Dating of Tepi-a Sopdet in Memphis''' |- {{Ägyptologie TblHighlight}} !Year ![[Culmination (astronomy)|Acronychal
Culmination]] ![[Acronychal|Acronychal
Setting]] ![[Heliacal rising|Heliacal
Rising]]The altitude values refer to the end of the respective night hour. Heliacal rising occurs before sunrise, acronychal setting, and acronychal culmination after sunset. !Remarks |- bgcolor="#fffaf0" | style="text-align:center" | 1873 BCE | style="text-align:center" | {{ill|Achet#Dating|lt=26th Achet II|de|26. Achet II}}
January 11–12
Altitude 30.3°Actual culmination: 36.5°, approximately 2.75 hours after sunset. | style="text-align:center" | {{ill|Peret#Dating|lt=26th Peret I|de|26. Peret I}}
April 11–12
Altitude 10.4°The observation of the setting was only possible towards the end of the decade. | style="text-align:center" | {{ill|Peret#Dating|lt=6th Peret IV|de|6. Peret IV}}
June 20–21
Altitude 2.3°On the 10th decade day, the altitude was 9.3°. A heliacal observation during the decade was therefore not possible. | style="text-align:center" | {{ill|Senusret III|de|Sesostris III.}} |- bgcolor="#f5f5f5" | style="text-align:center" | 1541 BCE | style="text-align:center" | 19th Peret I
January 14–15 | style="text-align:center" | 19th Peret IV
April 14–15 | style="text-align:center" | {{ill|Season of the Harvest|lt=Shemu#Dating|lt=29th Shemu II|de|29. Schemu II}}
June 23–24 | style="text-align:center" | {{ill|Ebers calendar|de|Ebers-Kalender}} |- bgcolor="#fffaf0" | style="text-align:center" | 1463 BCE | style="text-align:center" | 8th Peret II
January 15–16 | style="text-align:center" | 8th Shemu I
April 15–16 | style="text-align:center" | 18th Shemu III
June 24–25 | style="text-align:center" | {{ill|Ramesside star clocks|de|Ramessidische Sternuhren}} |- |} == Literature == * {{Ill|Christian Leitz|fr|Christian Leitz|en|Christian Leitz|de|Christian Leitz}}: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren''. Peeters, Leuven 1995, ISBN 90-6831-669-9, p. 95. * {{ill|Alexandra von Lieven|fr|Alexandra von Lieven|de|Alexandra von Lieven}}: ''Grundriss des Laufes der Sterne – Das sogenannte Nutbuch''. The Carsten Niebuhr Institute of Ancient Eastern Studies (et al.), Copenhagen 2007, ISBN 978-87-635-0406-5, pp. 62 and 398. == References == [[Category:Decan star (Ancient Egypt)]] [[Category:Ancient Egyptian Day]][[Category:Ancient Egyptian Day link incomplete]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:Tepi-a Sopdet]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:Ugoodbye $1 completion categories]] [[Category:qqqqqqqqqq]] [[Category:Tepi-a Sopdet]][[en:draft:Tepi-a Sopdet]] [[de:Tepi-a Sopdet]][[Category:Ugoodbye $1 completion categories]] [[Category:qqqqqqqqqq]] [[Category:Tepi-a Sopdet]][[Category:Ugoodbye $1 pages]] [[Category:Tepi-a Sopdet]][[Category:Ancient Egyptian Day]][[Category:Ancient Egyptian Day link incomplete]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:Tepi-a Sopdet]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:Ugoodbye $1 completion categories]] [[Category:qqqqqqqqqq]] [[Category:Tepi-a Sopdet]][[Category:bump 1-25]] [[Category:new pages]][[Category:Tepi-a Sopdet]][[Category:new pages]] [[Category:new pages]][[Category:Tepi-a Sopdet]][[Category:new pages]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Tepi-a-Sopdet]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Tepi-a-Sopdet]][[Category:qq]] [[sn:Tepi-a-Sopdet]] [[sn:Tepi-a-Sopdet]][[sn:Tepi-a-Sopdet]][[sn:Tepi-a-Sopdet]]

A heliacal observation during the decade was therefore not possible. | style="text-align:center" | {{ill|Senusret III|de|Sesostris III.}} |- bgcolor="#f5f5f5" | style="text-align:center" | 1541 BCE | style="text-align:center" | 19th Peret I
January 14–15 | style="text-align:center" | 19th Peret IV
April 14–15 | style="text-align:center" | {{ill|Season of the Harvest|lt=Shemu#Dating|lt=29th Shemu II|de|29. Schemu II}}
June 23–24 | style="text-align:center" | {{ill|Ebers calendar|de|Ebers-Kalender}} |- bgcolor="#fffaf0" | style="text-align:center" | 1463 BCE | style="text-align:center" | 8th Peret II
January 15–16 | style="text-align:center" | 8th Shemu I
April 15–16 | style="text-align:center" | 18th Shemu III
June 24–25 | style="text-align:center" | {{ill|Ramesside star clocks|de|Ramessidische Sternuhren}} |- |} == Literature == * {{Ill|Christian Leitz|fr|Christian Leitz|en|Christian Leitz|de|Christian Leitz}}: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren''. Peeters, Leuven 1995, ISBN 90-6831-669-9, p. 95. * {{ill|Alexandra von Lieven|fr|Alexandra von Lieven|de|Alexandra von Lieven}}: ''Grundriss des Laufes der Sterne – Das sogenannte Nutbuch''. revision.text:
{{Infobox Hieroglyphen |NAME = D1:D36 M44-X1 N14-N33 |NAME-TRANSKRIPTION = Tepi-a Sopdet
''Tpj-ˁ Spd.t''
''Forerunner of Sopdet'' |BILD1 = Position Beta Cma.png |BILD1-BREITE = 250px |BILD1-BESCHREIBUNG = Constellation “Sopdet” ([[Canis Major]]) }} '''Tepi-a Sopdet''' is the designation of an {{ill|Ancient Egypt|de|Altes Ägypten}} {{ill|Decade (Ancient Egypt)|de|Dekade (Altägypten)|1=Decan}}, which comprised only one {{ill|Baktiu|de|Baktiu}} (decan star). == Background == This decan corresponds to {{ill|Murzim|de|Murzim}}, the forerunner of {{ill|Sirius|de|Sirius}}. The traditional {{ill|Arabic language|de|Arabische Sprache}} name, "Harbinger," likely refers to its position, as it rises before Sirius and heralds the brightest star in the night sky. However, this role does not apply to {{ill|Heliacal rising|de|Heliakisch}} because Murzim is significantly dimmer than Sirius and requires a {{ill|Altitude|de|Sehungsbogen}} of at least 13° to become heliacally visible, or approximately 55 minutes before the sun rises. Furthermore, Murzim requires a minimum horizon altitude of 1.7° due to atmospheric refraction. The same conditions apply to {{ill|Acronychal setting|de|Akronychisch}}. The entries in the {{ill|Book of Nut|de|Nutbuch}} regarding decan positions can therefore only refer to the acronychal {{ill|Culmination (astronomy)|de|Kulmination (Astronomie)}}, as only in this context could Murzim rank before Sirius in the decan lists. During the era of the {{ill|Diagonal star clock|de|Diagonalsternuhren}}, Murzim reached a culmination altitude of approximately 36.6° in Ancient Egypt. Sirius itself was part of the Ancient Egyptian constellation {{ill|Sopdet (constellation)|de|Sopdet (Sternbild)}}. {{ill|Alexandra von Lieven|fr|Alexandra von Lieven|de|Alexandra von Lieven}} notes the decan star "Tepi-a Sopdet" mentioned in the {{ill|Book of Nut|de|Nutbuch}}, which in the seventh year of {{ill|Senusret III|de|Sesostris III.}} was listed as the 34th decan with the {{ill|Heliacal rising|de|Heliakisch}} assigned to the {{ill|Peret#Dating|lt=6th Peret IV|de|6. Peret IV}} (approximately June 15/21); the {{ill|Culmination (astronomy)|de|Kulmination (Astronomie)}} for the {{ill|Achet#Dating|lt=26th Achet II|de|26. Achet II}}, and the acronychal setting for the 26th Peret I. The recorded period of invisibility lasted the canonical 70 days.Alexandra von Lieven: ''Grundriss des Laufes der Sterne''. p. 66. == Dating of Tepi-a Sopdet (Book of Nut System) == {| class="wikitable" style="width: 98%; border: 2px solid gray;" |- {{Ägyptologie TblHighlight}} |colspan="7" align=center style="background:#FFE39B"|'''Dating of Tepi-a Sopdet in Memphis''' |- {{Ägyptologie TblHighlight}} !Year ![[Culmination (astronomy)|Acronychal
Culmination]] ![[Acronychal|Acronychal
Setting]] ![[Heliacal rising|Heliacal
Rising]]The altitude values refer to the end of the respective night hour. Heliacal rising occurs before sunrise, acronychal setting, and acronychal culmination after sunset. !Remarks |- bgcolor="#fffaf0" | style="text-align:center" | 1873 BCE | style="text-align:center" | {{ill|Achet#Dating|lt=26th Achet II|de|26. Achet II}}
January 11–12
Altitude 30.3°Actual culmination: 36.5°, approximately 2.75 hours after sunset. | style="text-align:center" | {{ill|Peret#Dating|lt=26th Peret I|de|26. Peret I}}
April 11–12
Altitude 10.4°The observation of the setting was only possible towards the end of the decade. | style="text-align:center" | {{ill|Peret#Dating|lt=6th Peret IV|de|6. Peret IV}}
June 20–21
Altitude 2.3°On the 10th decade day, the altitude was 9.3°. A heliacal observation during the decade was therefore not possible. | style="text-align:center" | {{ill|Senusret III|de|Sesostris III.}} |- bgcolor="#f5f5f5" | style="text-align:center" | 1541 BCE | style="text-align:center" | 19th Peret I
January 14–15 | style="text-align:center" | 19th Peret IV
April 14–15 | style="text-align:center" | {{ill|Season of the Harvest|lt=Shemu#Dating|lt=29th Shemu II|de|29. Schemu II}}
June 23–24 | style="text-align:center" | {{ill|Ebers calendar|de|Ebers-Kalender}} |- bgcolor="#fffaf0" | style="text-align:center" | 1463 BCE | style="text-align:center" | 8th Peret II
January 15–16 | style="text-align:center" | 8th Shemu I
April 15–16 | style="text-align:center" | 18th Shemu III
June 24–25 | style="text-align:center" | {{ill|Ramesside star clocks|de|Ramessidische Sternuhren}} |- |} == Literature == * {{Ill|Christian Leitz|fr|Christian Leitz|en|Christian Leitz|de|Christian Leitz}}: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren''. Peeters, Leuven 1995, ISBN 90-6831-669-9, p. 95. * {{ill|Alexandra von Lieven|fr|Alexandra von Lieven|de|Alexandra von Lieven}}: ''Grundriss des Laufes der Sterne – Das sogenannte Nutbuch''. The Carsten Niebuhr Institute of Ancient Eastern Studies (et al.), Copenhagen 2007, ISBN 978-87-635-0406-5, pp. 62 and 398. == References == [[Category:Decan star (Ancient Egypt)]] [[Category:Ancient Egyptian Day]][[Category:Ancient Egyptian Day link incomplete]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:Tepi-a Sopdet]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:Tepi-a Sopdet]][[en:draft:Tepi-a Sopdet]] [[de:Tepi-a Sopdet]][[Category:Tepi-a Sopdet]][[Category:Ugoodbye $1 pages]] [[Category:Tepi-a Sopdet]][[Category:Ancient Egyptian Day]][[Category:Ancient Egyptian Day link incomplete]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:Tepi-a Sopdet]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:Tepi-a Sopdet]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:Tepi-a Sopdet]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:Tepi-a Sopdet]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Tepi-a-Sopdet]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Tepi-a-Sopdet]][[Category:qq]] [[sn:Tepi-a-Sopdet]] [[sn:Tepi-a-Sopdet]][[sn:Tepi-a-Sopdet]][[sn:Tepi-a-Sopdet]]

A heliacal observation during the decade was therefore not possible. | style="text-align:center" | {{ill|Senusret III|de|Sesostris III.}} |- bgcolor="#f5f5f5" | style="text-align:center" | 1541 BCE | style="text-align:center" | 19th Peret I
January 14–15 | style="text-align:center" | 19th Peret IV
April 14–15 | style="text-align:center" | {{ill|Season of the Harvest|lt=Shemu#Dating|lt=29th Shemu II|de|29. Schemu II}}
June 23–24 | style="text-align:center" | {{ill|Ebers calendar|de|Ebers-Kalender}} |- bgcolor="#fffaf0" | style="text-align:center" | 1463 BCE | style="text-align:center" | 8th Peret II
January 15–16 | style="text-align:center" | 8th Shemu I
April 15–16 | style="text-align:center" | 18th Shemu III

revision.text:
{{Infobox Hieroglyphen |NAME = D1:D36 M44-X1 N14-N33 |NAME-TRANSKRIPTION = Tepi-a Sopdet
''Tpj-ˁ Spd.t''
''Forerunner of Sopdet'' |BILD1 = Position Beta Cma.png |BILD1-BREITE = 250px |BILD1-BESCHREIBUNG = Constellation “Sopdet” ([[Canis Major]]) }} '''Tepi-a Sopdet''' is the designation of an {{ill|Ancient Egypt|de|Altes Ägypten}} {{ill|Decade (Ancient Egypt)|de|Dekade (Altägypten)|1=Decan}}, which comprised only one {{ill|Baktiu|de|Baktiu}} (decan star). == Background == This decan corresponds to {{ill|Murzim|de|Murzim}}, the forerunner of {{ill|Sirius|de|Sirius}}. The traditional {{ill|Arabic language|de|Arabische Sprache}} name, "Harbinger," likely refers to its position, as it rises before Sirius and heralds the brightest star in the night sky. However, this role does not apply to {{ill|Heliacal rising|de|Heliakisch}} because Murzim is significantly dimmer than Sirius and requires a {{ill|Altitude|de|Sehungsbogen}} of at least 13° to become heliacally visible, or approximately 55 minutes before the sun rises. Furthermore, Murzim requires a minimum horizon altitude of 1.7° due to atmospheric refraction. The same conditions apply to {{ill|Acronychal setting|de|Akronychisch}}. The entries in the {{ill|Book of Nut|de|Nutbuch}} regarding decan positions can therefore only refer to the acronychal {{ill|Culmination (astronomy)|de|Kulmination (Astronomie)}}, as only in this context could Murzim rank before Sirius in the decan lists. During the era of the {{ill|Diagonal star clock|de|Diagonalsternuhren}}, Murzim reached a culmination altitude of approximately 36.6° in Ancient Egypt. Sirius itself was part of the Ancient Egyptian constellation {{ill|Sopdet (constellation)|de|Sopdet (Sternbild)}}. {{ill|Alexandra von Lieven|fr|Alexandra von Lieven|de|Alexandra von Lieven}} notes the decan star "Tepi-a Sopdet" mentioned in the {{ill|Book of Nut|de|Nutbuch}}, which in the seventh year of {{ill|Senusret III|de|Sesostris III.}} was listed as the 34th decan with the {{ill|Heliacal rising|de|Heliakisch}} assigned to the {{ill|Peret#Dating|lt=6th Peret IV|de|6. Peret IV}} (approximately June 15/21); the {{ill|Culmination (astronomy)|de|Kulmination (Astronomie)}} for the {{ill|Achet#Dating|lt=26th Achet II|de|26. Achet II}}, and the acronychal setting for the 26th Peret I. The recorded period of invisibility lasted the canonical 70 days.Alexandra von Lieven: ''Grundriss des Laufes der Sterne''. p. 66. == Dating of Tepi-a Sopdet (Book of Nut System) == {| class="wikitable" style="width: 98%; border: 2px solid gray;" |- {{Ägyptologie TblHighlight}} |colspan="7" align=center style="background:#FFE39B"|'''Dating of Tepi-a Sopdet in Memphis''' |- {{Ägyptologie TblHighlight}} !Year ![[Culmination (astronomy)|Acronychal
Culmination]] ![[Acronychal|Acronychal
Setting]] ![[Heliacal rising|Heliacal
Rising]]The altitude values refer to the end of the respective night hour. Heliacal rising occurs before sunrise, acronychal setting, and acronychal culmination after sunset. !Remarks |- bgcolor="#fffaf0" | style="text-align:center" | 1873 BCE | style="text-align:center" | {{ill|Achet#Dating|lt=26th Achet II|de|26. Achet II}}
January 11–12
Altitude 30.3°Actual culmination: 36.5°, approximately 2.75 hours after sunset. | style="text-align:center" | {{ill|Peret#Dating|lt=26th Peret I|de|26. Peret I}}
April 11–12
Altitude 10.4°The observation of the setting was only possible towards the end of the decade. | style="text-align:center" | {{ill|Peret#Dating|lt=6th Peret IV|de|6. Peret IV}}
June 20–21
Altitude 2.3°On the 10th decade day, the altitude was 9.3°. A heliacal observation during the decade was therefore not possible. | style="text-align:center" | {{ill|Senusret III|de|Sesostris III.}} |- bgcolor="#f5f5f5" | style="text-align:center" | 1541 BCE | style="text-align:center" | 19th Peret I
January 14–15 | style="text-align:center" | 19th Peret IV
April 14–15 | style="text-align:center" | {{ill|Season of the Harvest|lt=Shemu#Dating|lt=29th Shemu II|de|29. Schemu II}}
June 23–24 | style="text-align:center" | {{ill|Ebers calendar|de|Ebers-Kalender}} |- bgcolor="#fffaf0" | style="text-align:center" | 1463 BCE | style="text-align:center" | 8th Peret II
January 15–16 | style="text-align:center" | 8th Shemu I
April 15–16 | style="text-align:center" | 18th Shemu III
June 24–25 | style="text-align:center" | {{ill|Ramesside star clocks|de|Ramessidische Sternuhren}} |- |} == Literature == * {{Ill|Christian Leitz|fr|Christian Leitz|en|Christian Leitz|de|Christian Leitz}}: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren''. Peeters, Leuven 1995, ISBN 90-6831-669-9, p. 95. * {{ill|Alexandra von Lieven|fr|Alexandra von Lieven|de|Alexandra von Lieven}}: ''Grundriss des Laufes der Sterne – Das sogenannte Nutbuch''. The Carsten Niebuhr Institute of Ancient Eastern Studies (et al.), Copenhagen 2007, ISBN 978-87-635-0406-5, pp. 62 and 398. == References == [[Category:Decan star (Ancient Egypt)]] [[Category:Ancient Egyptian Day]][[Category:Ancient Egyptian Day link incomplete]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:Tepi-a Sopdet]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:Tepi-a Sopdet]][[en:draft:Tepi-a Sopdet]] [[de:Tepi-a Sopdet]][[Category:Tepi-a Sopdet]][[Category:Ugoodbye $1 pages]] [[Category:Tepi-a Sopdet]][[Category:Ancient Egyptian Day]][[Category:Ancient Egyptian Day link incomplete]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:Tepi-a Sopdet]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:Tepi-a Sopdet]][[Category:Ugoodbye $1 completion categories]] [[Category:Tepi-a Sopdet]][[Category:Ugoodbye $1 completion categories]] [[Category:Ugoodbye $1 completion categories]] [[Category:Tepi-a Sopdet]][[Category:Ugoodbye $1 completion categories]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Tepi-a-Sopdet]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Tepi-a-Sopdet]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Tepi-a-Sopdet]][[Category:qq]] [[sn:Tepi-a-Sopdet]] [[sn:Tepi-a-Sopdet]][[sn:Tepi-a-Sopdet]][[sn:Tepi-a-Sopdet]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Tepi-a-Sopdet}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Tepi-a-Sopdet}}

revision.text:
{{Infobox Hieroglyphen |NAME = D1:D36 M44-X1 N14-N33 |NAME-TRANSKRIPTION = Tepi-a Sopdet
''Tpj-ˁ Spd.t''
''Forerunner of Sopdet'' |BILD1 = Position Beta Cma.png |BILD1-BREITE = 250px |BILD1-BESCHREIBUNG = Constellation “Sopdet” ([[Canis Major]]) }} '''Tepi-a Sopdet''' is the designation of an {{ill|Ancient Egypt|de|Altes Ägypten}} {{ill|Decade (Ancient Egypt)|de|Dekade (Altägypten)|1=Decan}}, which comprised only one {{ill|Baktiu|de|Baktiu}} (decan star). == Background == This decan corresponds to {{ill|Murzim|de|Murzim}}, the forerunner of {{ill|Sirius|de|Sirius}}. The traditional {{ill|Arabic language|de|Arabische Sprache}} name, "Harbinger," likely refers to its position, as it rises before Sirius and heralds the brightest star in the night sky. However, this role does not apply to {{ill|Heliacal rising|de|Heliakisch}} because Murzim is significantly dimmer than Sirius and requires a {{ill|Altitude|de|Sehungsbogen}} of at least 13° to become heliacally visible, or approximately 55 minutes before the sun rises. Furthermore, Murzim requires a minimum horizon altitude of 1.7° due to atmospheric refraction. The same conditions apply to {{ill|Acronychal setting|de|Akronychisch}}. The entries in the {{ill|Book of Nut|de|Nutbuch}} regarding decan positions can therefore only refer to the acronychal {{ill|Culmination (astronomy)|de|Kulmination (Astronomie)}}, as only in this context could Murzim rank before Sirius in the decan lists. During the era of the {{ill|Diagonal star clock|de|Diagonalsternuhren}}, Murzim reached a culmination altitude of approximately 36.6° in Ancient Egypt. Sirius itself was part of the Ancient Egyptian constellation {{ill|Sopdet (constellation)|de|Sopdet (Sternbild)}}. {{ill|Alexandra von Lieven|fr|Alexandra von Lieven|de|Alexandra von Lieven}} notes the decan star "Tepi-a Sopdet" mentioned in the {{ill|Book of Nut|de|Nutbuch}}, which in the seventh year of {{ill|Senusret III|de|Sesostris III.}} was listed as the 34th decan with the {{ill|Heliacal rising|de|Heliakisch}} assigned to the {{ill|Peret#Dating|lt=6th Peret IV|de|6. Peret IV}} (approximately June 15/21); the {{ill|Culmination (astronomy)|de|Kulmination (Astronomie)}} for the {{ill|Achet#Dating|lt=26th Achet II|de|26. Achet II}}, and the acronychal setting for the 26th Peret I. The recorded period of invisibility lasted the canonical 70 days.Alexandra von Lieven: ''Grundriss des Laufes der Sterne''. p. 66. == Dating of Tepi-a Sopdet (Book of Nut System) == {| class="wikitable" style="width: 98%; border: 2px solid gray;" |- {{Ägyptologie TblHighlight}} |colspan="7" align=center style="background:#FFE39B"|'''Dating of Tepi-a Sopdet in Memphis''' |- {{Ägyptologie TblHighlight}} !Year ![[Culmination (astronomy)|Acronychal
Culmination]] ![[Acronychal|Acronychal
Setting]] ![[Heliacal rising|Heliacal
Rising]]The altitude values refer to the end of the respective night hour. Heliacal rising occurs before sunrise, acronychal setting, and acronychal culmination after sunset. !Remarks |- bgcolor="#fffaf0" | style="text-align:center" | 1873 BCE | style="text-align:center" | {{ill|Achet#Dating|lt=26th Achet II|de|26. Achet II}}
January 11–12
Altitude 30.3°Actual culmination: 36.5°, approximately 2.75 hours after sunset. | style="text-align:center" | {{ill|Peret#Dating|lt=26th Peret I|de|26. Peret I}}
April 11–12
Altitude 10.4°The observation of the setting was only possible towards the end of the decade. | style="text-align:center" | {{ill|Peret#Dating|lt=6th Peret IV|de|6. Peret IV}}
June 20–21
Altitude 2.3°On the 10th decade day, the altitude was 9.3°. A heliacal observation during the decade was therefore not possible. | style="text-align:center" | {{ill|Senusret III|de|Sesostris III.}} |- bgcolor="#f5f5f5" | style="text-align:center" | 1541 BCE | style="text-align:center" | 19th Peret I
January 14–15 | style="text-align:center" | 19th Peret IV
April 14–15 | style="text-align:center" | {{ill|Season of the Harvest|lt=Shemu#Dating|lt=29th Shemu II|de|29. Schemu II}}
June 23–24 | style="text-align:center" | {{ill|Ebers calendar|de|Ebers-Kalender}} |- bgcolor="#fffaf0" | style="text-align:center" | 1463 BCE | style="text-align:center" | 8th Peret II
January 15–16 | style="text-align:center" | 8th Shemu I
April 15–16 | style="text-align:center" | 18th Shemu III
June 24–25 | style="text-align:center" | {{ill|Ramesside star clocks|de|Ramessidische Sternuhren}} |- |} == Literature == * {{Interlanguage link|12=draft|Christian Leitz|fr|Christian Leitz|en|Christian Leitz|de|Christian Leitz}}: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren''. Peeters, Leuven 1995, ISBN 90-6831-669-9, p. 95. * {{ill|Alexandra von Lieven|fr|Alexandra von Lieven|de|Alexandra von Lieven}}: ''Grundriss des Laufes der Sterne – Das sogenannte Nutbuch''. The Carsten Niebuhr Institute of Ancient Eastern Studies (et al.), Copenhagen 2007, ISBN 978-87-635-0406-5, pp. 62 and 398. == References == [[Category:Decan star (Ancient Egypt)]] [[Category:Ancient Egyptian Day]][[Category:Ancient Egyptian Day link incomplete]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:Tepi-a Sopdet]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:Tepi-a Sopdet]][[en:draft:Tepi-a Sopdet]] [[de:Tepi-a Sopdet]][[Category:Tepi-a Sopdet]][[Category:Tepi-a Sopdet]][[Category:Ancient Egyptian Day]][[Category:Ancient Egyptian Day link incomplete]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:Tepi-a Sopdet]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:Tepi-a Sopdet]][[Category:Tepi-a Sopdet]][[Category:Tepi-a Sopdet]][[Category:11]][[sn:Tepi-a-Sopdet]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Tepi-a-Sopdet]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Tepi-a-Sopdet]][[Category:11]] [[sn:Tepi-a-Sopdet]] [[sn:Tepi-a-Sopdet]][[sn:Tepi-a-Sopdet]][[sn:Tepi-a-Sopdet]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Tepi-a-Sopdet}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Tepi-a-Sopdet}}

The recorded period of invisibility lasted the canonical 70 days.Alexandra von Lieven: ''Grundriss des Laufes der Sterne''. p. 66. == Dating of Tepi-a Sopdet (Book of Nut System) == {| class="wikitable" style="width: 98%; border: 2px solid gray;" |- {{Ägyptologie TblHighlight}} |colspan="7" align=center style="background:#FFE39B"|'''Dating of Tepi-a Sopdet in Memphis''' |- {{Ägyptologie TblHighlight}} !Year ![[Culmination (astronomy)|Acronychal
Culmination]] ![[Acronychal|Acronychal
Setting]] ![[Heliacal rising|Heliacal
Rising]]The altitude values refer to the end of the respective night hour. Heliacal rising occurs before sunrise, acronychal setting, and acronychal culmination after sunset. !Remarks |- bgcolor="#fffaf0" | style="text-align:center" | 1873 BCE | style="text-align:center" | {{ill|Achet#Dating|lt=26th Achet II|de|26. Achet II}}
January 11–12
Altitude 30.3°Actual culmination: 36.5°, approximately 2.75 hours after sunset. | style="text-align:center" | {{ill|Peret#Dating|lt=26th Peret I|de|26. Peret I}}
April 11–12
Altitude 10.4°The observation of the setting was only possible towards the end of the decade. | style="text-align:center" | {{ill|Peret#Dating|lt=6th Peret IV|de|6. Peret IV}}
June 20–21
Altitude 2.3°On the 10th decade day, the altitude was 9.3°. A heliacal observation during the decade was therefore not possible. | style="text-align:center" | {{ill|Senusret III|de|Sesostris III.}} |- bgcolor="#f5f5f5" | style="text-align:center" | 1541 BCE | style="text-align:center" | 19th Peret I
January 14–15 | style="text-align:center" | 19th Peret IV
April 14–15 | style="text-align:center" | {{ill|Season of the Harvest|lt=Shemu#Dating|lt=29th Shemu II|de|29. Schemu II}}
June 23–24 | style="text-align:center" | {{ill|Ebers calendar|de|Ebers-Kalender}} |- bgcolor="#fffaf0" | style="text-align:center" | 1463 BCE | style="text-align:center" | 8th Peret II
January 15–16 | style="text-align:center" | 8th Shemu I
April 15–16 | style="text-align:center" | 18th Shemu III
June 24–25 | style="text-align:center" | {{ill|Ramesside star clocks|de|Ramessidische Sternuhren}} |- |} == Literature == * {{Interlanguage link|12=draft|Christian Leitz|fr|Christian Leitz|en|Christian Leitz|de|Christian Leitz}}: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren''. Peeters, Leuven 1995, ISBN 90-6831-669-9, p. 95. * {{ill|Alexandra von Lieven|fr|Alexandra von Lieven|de|Alexandra von Lieven}}: ''Grundriss des Laufes der Sterne – Das sogenannte Nutbuch''. {{Infobox Hieroglyphen |NAME = D1:D36 M44-X1 N14-N33 |NAME-TRANSKRIPTION = Tepi-a Sopdet
''Tpj-ˁ Spd.t''
''Forerunner of Sopdet'' |BILD1 = Position Beta Cma.png |BILD1-BREITE = 250px |BILD1-BESCHREIBUNG = Constellation “Sopdet” ([[Canis Major]]) }} '''Tepi-a Sopdet''' is the designation of an {{ill|Ancient Egypt|de|Altes Ägypten}} {{ill|Decade (Ancient Egypt)|de|Dekade (Altägypten)|1=Decan}}, which comprised only one {{ill|Baktiu|de|Baktiu}} (decan star). == Background == This decan corresponds to {{ill|Murzim|de|Murzim}}, the forerunner of {{ill|Sirius|de|Sirius}}. The traditional {{ill|Arabic language|de|Arabische Sprache}} name, "Harbinger," likely refers to its position, as it rises before Sirius and heralds the brightest star in the night sky. However, this role does not apply to {{ill|Heliacal rising|de|Heliakisch}} because Murzim is significantly dimmer than Sirius and requires a {{ill|Altitude|de|Sehungsbogen}} of at least 13° to become heliacally visible, or approximately 55 minutes before the sun rises. Furthermore, Murzim requires a minimum horizon altitude of 1.7° due to atmospheric refraction. The same conditions apply to {{ill|Acronychal setting|de|Akronychisch}}. The entries in the {{ill|Book of Nut|de|Nutbuch}} regarding decan positions can therefore only refer to the acronychal {{ill|Culmination (astronomy)|de|Kulmination (Astronomie)}}, as only in this context could Murzim rank before Sirius in the decan lists. During the era of the {{ill|Diagonal star clock|de|Diagonalsternuhren}}, Murzim reached a culmination altitude of approximately 36.6° in Ancient Egypt. Sirius itself was part of the Ancient Egyptian constellation {{ill|Sopdet (constellation)|de|Sopdet (Sternbild)}}. {{ill|Alexandra von Lieven|fr|Alexandra von Lieven|de|Alexandra von Lieven}} notes the decan star "Tepi-a Sopdet" mentioned in the {{ill|Book of Nut|de|Nutbuch}}, which in the seventh year of {{ill|Senusret III|de|Sesostris III.}} was listed as the 34th decan with the {{ill|Heliacal rising|de|Heliakisch}} assigned to the {{ill|Peret#Dating|lt=6th Peret IV|de|6. Peret IV}} (approximately June 15/21); the {{ill|Culmination (astronomy)|de|Kulmination (Astronomie)}} for the {{ill|Achet#Dating|lt=26th Achet II|de|26. Achet II}}, and the acronychal setting for the 26th Peret I. The recorded period of invisibility lasted the canonical 70 days.Alexandra von Lieven: ''Grundriss des Laufes der Sterne''. p. 66. == Dating of Tepi-a Sopdet (Book of Nut System) == {| class="wikitable" style="width: 98%; border: 2px solid gray;" |- {{Ägyptologie TblHighlight}} |colspan="7" align=center style="background:#FFE39B"|'''Dating of Tepi-a Sopdet in Memphis''' |- {{Ägyptologie TblHighlight}} !Year ![[Culmination (astronomy)|Acronychal
Culmination]] ![[Acronychal|Acronychal
Setting]] ![[Heliacal rising|Heliacal
Rising]]The altitude values refer to the end of the respective night hour. Heliacal rising occurs before sunrise, acronychal setting, and acronychal culmination after sunset. !Remarks |- bgcolor="#fffaf0" | style="text-align:center" | 1873 BCE | style="text-align:center" | {{ill|Achet#Dating|lt=26th Achet II|de|26. Achet II}}
January 11–12
Altitude 30.3°Actual culmination: 36.5°, approximately 2.75 hours after sunset. | style="text-align:center" | {{ill|Peret#Dating|lt=26th Peret I|de|26. Peret I}}
April 11–12
Altitude 10.4°The observation of the setting was only possible towards the end of the decade. | style="text-align:center" | {{ill|Peret#Dating|lt=6th Peret IV|de|6. Peret IV}}
June 20–21
Altitude 2.3°On the 10th decade day, the altitude was 9.3°. A heliacal observation during the decade was therefore not possible. | style="text-align:center" | {{ill|Senusret III|de|Sesostris III.}} |- bgcolor="#f5f5f5" | style="text-align:center" | 1541 BCE | style="text-align:center" | 19th Peret I
January 14–15 | style="text-align:center" | 19th Peret IV
April 14–15 | style="text-align:center" | {{ill|Season of the Harvest|lt=Shemu#Dating|lt=29th Shemu II|de|29. Schemu II}}
June 23–24 | style="text-align:center" | {{ill|Ebers calendar|de|Ebers-Kalender}} |- bgcolor="#fffaf0" | style="text-align:center" | 1463 BCE | style="text-align:center" | 8th Peret II
January 15–16 | style="text-align:center" | 8th Shemu I
April 15–16 | style="text-align:center" | 18th Shemu III
June 24–25 | style="text-align:center" | {{ill|Ramesside star clocks|de|Ramessidische Sternuhren}} |- |} == Literature == * {{Interlanguage link|12=draft|Christian Leitz|fr|Christian Leitz|en|Christian Leitz|de|Christian Leitz}}: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren''. Peeters, Leuven 1995, ISBN 90-6831-669-9, p. 95. * {{ill|Alexandra von Lieven|fr|Alexandra von Lieven|de|Alexandra von Lieven}}: ''Grundriss des Laufes der Sterne – Das sogenannte Nutbuch''. The Carsten Niebuhr Institute of Ancient Eastern Studies (et al.), Copenhagen 2007, ISBN 978-87-635-0406-5, pp. 62 and 398. == References == [[Category:Decan star (Ancient Egypt)]] [[Category:Ancient Egyptian Day]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:Tepi-a Sopdet]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:Tepi-a Sopdet]][[en:draft:Tepi-a Sopdet]] [[de:Tepi-a Sopdet]][[Category:Tepi-a Sopdet]][[Category:Tepi-a Sopdet]][[Category:Ancient Egyptian Day]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:Tepi-a Sopdet]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:Tepi-a Sopdet]][[Category:Tepi-a Sopdet]][[Category:Tepi-a Sopdet]][[Category:11]][[sn:Tepi-a-Sopdet]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Tepi-a-Sopdet]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Tepi-a-Sopdet]][[Category:11]] [[Category:Ancient Egyptian Day]] [[sn:Tepi-a-Sopdet]][[Category:Ancient Egyptian Day]] [[sn:Tepi-a-Sopdet]] [[sn:Tepi-a-Sopdet]][[sn:Tepi-a-Sopdet]][[sn:Tepi-a-Sopdet]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Tepi-a-Sopdet}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Tepi-a-Sopdet}}

The entries in the {{ill|Book of Nut|de|Nutbuch|12=draft}} regarding decan positions can therefore only refer to the acronychal {{ill|Culmination (astronomy)|de|Kulmination (Astronomie)|12=draft}}, as only in this context could Murzim rank before Sirius in the decan lists. During the era of the {{ill|Diagonal star clock|de|Diagonalsternuhren|12=draft}}, Murzim reached a culmination altitude of approximately 36.6° in Ancient Egypt. Sirius itself was part of the Ancient Egyptian constellation {{ill|Sopdet (constellation)|de|Sopdet (Sternbild)|12=draft}}. {{ill|Alexandra von Lieven|fr|Alexandra von Lieven|de|Alexandra von Lieven|12=draft}} notes the decan star "Tepi-a Sopdet" mentioned in the {{ill|Book of Nut|de|Nutbuch|12=draft}}, which in the seventh year of {{ill|Senusret III|de|Sesostris III.|12=draft}} was listed as the 34th decan with the {{ill|Heliacal rising|de|Heliakisch|12=draft}} assigned to the {{ill|Peret#Dating|lt=6th Peret IV|de|6. Peret IV|12=draft}} (approximately June 15/21); the {{ill|Culmination (astronomy)|de|Kulmination (Astronomie)|12=draft}} for the {{ill|Achet#Dating|lt=26th Achet II|de|26. Achet II|12=draft}}, and the acronychal setting for the 26th Peret I. The recorded period of invisibility lasted the canonical 70 days.Alexandra von Lieven: ''Grundriss des Laufes der Sterne''. p. 66. == Dating of Tepi-a Sopdet (Book of Nut System) == {| class="wikitable" style="width: 98%; border: 2px solid gray;" |- {{Ägyptologie TblHighlight}} |colspan="7" align=center style="background:#FFE39B"|'''Dating of Tepi-a Sopdet in Memphis''' |- {{Ägyptologie TblHighlight}} !Year ![[Culmination (astronomy)|Acronychal
Culmination]] ![[Acronychal|Acronychal
Setting]] ![[Heliacal rising|Heliacal
Rising]]The altitude values refer to the end of the respective night hour. Heliacal rising occurs before sunrise, acronychal setting, and acronychal culmination after sunset. !Remarks |- bgcolor="#fffaf0" | style="text-align:center" | 1873 BCE | style="text-align:center" | {{ill|Achet#Dating|lt=26th Achet II|de|26. Achet II|12=draft}}
January 11–12
Altitude 30.3°Actual culmination: 36.5°, approximately 2.75 hours after sunset. | style="text-align:center" | {{ill|Peret#Dating|lt=26th Peret I|de|26. Peret I|12=draft}}
April 11–12
Altitude 10.4°The observation of the setting was only possible towards the end of the decade. | style="text-align:center" | {{ill|Peret#Dating|lt=6th Peret IV|de|6. Peret IV|12=draft}}
June 20–21
Altitude 2.3°On the 10th decade day, the altitude was 9.3°. A heliacal observation during the decade was therefore not possible. | style="text-align:center" | {{ill|Senusret III|de|Sesostris III.|12=draft}} |- bgcolor="#f5f5f5" | style="text-align:center" | 1541 BCE | style="text-align:center" | 19th Peret I
January 14–15 | style="text-align:center" | 19th Peret IV
April 14–15 | style="text-align:center" | {{ill|Season of the Harvest|lt=Shemu#Dating|lt=29th Shemu II|de|29. Schemu II|12=draft}}
June 23–24 | style="text-align:center" | {{ill|Ebers calendar|de|Ebers-Kalender|12=draft}} |- bgcolor="#fffaf0" | style="text-align:center" | 1463 BCE | style="text-align:center" | 8th Peret II
January 15–16 | style="text-align:center" | 8th Shemu I
April 15–16 | style="text-align:center" | 18th Shemu III
June 24–25 | style="text-align:center" | {{ill|Ramesside star clocks|de|Ramessidische Sternuhren|12=draft}} |- |} == Literature == * {{Interlanguage link|12=draft|Christian Leitz|fr|Christian Leitz|en|Christian Leitz|de|Christian Leitz}}: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren''. Peeters, Leuven 1995, ISBN 90-6831-669-9, p. 95. * {{ill|Alexandra von Lieven|fr|Alexandra von Lieven|de|Alexandra von Lieven|12=draft}}: ''Grundriss des Laufes der Sterne – Das sogenannte Nutbuch''. The Carsten Niebuhr Institute of Ancient Eastern Studies (et al.), Copenhagen 2007, ISBN 978-87-635-0406-5, pp. 62 and 398. == References == {{draft categories| [[Category:Decan star (Ancient Egypt)]] [[Category:Ancient Egyptian Day]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:Tepi-a Sopdet]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:pages which might possibly be incomplete but are most likely complete]] [[Category:I think I cleared all the incomplete or blank pages earlier]] }} [[en:draft:Tepi-a Sopdet]] [[de:Tepi-a Sopdet]] [[sn:Tepi-a-Sopdet]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Tepi-a-Sopdet}}

{{Infobox Hieroglyphen |NAME = D1:D36 M44-X1 N14-N33 |NAME-TRANSKRIPTION = Tepi-a Sopdet
''Tpj-ˁ Spd.t''
''Forerunner of Sopdet'' |BILD1 = Position Beta Cma.png |BILD1-BREITE = 250px |BILD1-BESCHREIBUNG = Constellation “Sopdet” ([[Canis Major]]) }} '''Tepi-a Sopdet''' is the designation of an {{ill|Ancient Egypt|de|Altes Ägypten|12=draft}} {{ill|Decade (Ancient Egypt)|de|Dekade (Altägypten)|1=Decan|12=draft}}, which comprised only one {{ill|Baktiu|de|Baktiu|12=draft}} (decan star). == Background == This decan corresponds to {{ill|Murzim|de|Murzim|12=draft}}, the forerunner of {{ill|Sirius|de|Sirius|12=draft}}. The traditional {{ill|Arabic language|de|Arabische Sprache|12=draft}} name, "Harbinger," likely refers to its position, as it rises before Sirius and heralds the brightest star in the night sky. However, this role does not apply to {{ill|Heliacal rising|de|Heliakisch|12=draft}} because Murzim is significantly dimmer than Sirius and requires a {{ill|Altitude|de|Sehungsbogen|12=draft}} of at least 13° to become heliacally visible, or approximately 55 minutes before the sun rises. Furthermore, Murzim requires a minimum horizon altitude of 1.7° due to atmospheric refraction. The same conditions apply to {{ill|Acronychal setting|de|Akronychisch|12=draft}}. The entries in the {{ill|Book of Nut|de|Nutbuch|12=draft}} regarding decan positions can therefore only refer to the acronychal {{ill|Culmination (astronomy)|de|Kulmination (Astronomie)|12=draft}}, as only in this context could Murzim rank before Sirius in the decan lists. During the era of the {{ill|Diagonal star clock|de|Diagonalsternuhren|12=draft}}, Murzim reached a culmination altitude of approximately 36.6° in Ancient Egypt. Sirius itself was part of the Ancient Egyptian constellation {{ill|Sopdet (constellation)|de|Sopdet (Sternbild)|12=draft}}. {{ill|Alexandra von Lieven|fr|Alexandra von Lieven|de|Alexandra von Lieven|12=draft}} notes the decan star "Tepi-a Sopdet" mentioned in the {{ill|Book of Nut|de|Nutbuch|12=draft}}, which in the seventh year of {{ill|Senusret III|de|Sesostris III.|12=draft}} was listed as the 34th decan with the {{ill|Heliacal rising|de|Heliakisch|12=draft}} assigned to the {{ill|Peret#Dating|lt=6th Peret IV|de|6. Peret IV|12=draft}} (approximately June 15/21); the {{ill|Culmination (astronomy)|de|Kulmination (Astronomie)|12=draft}} for the {{ill|Achet#Dating|lt=26th Achet II|de|26. Achet II|12=draft}}, and the acronychal setting for the 26th Peret I. The recorded period of invisibility lasted the canonical 70 days.Alexandra von Lieven: ''Grundriss des Laufes der Sterne''. p. 66. == Dating of Tepi-a Sopdet (Book of Nut System) == {| class="wikitable" style="width: 98%; border: 2px solid gray;" |- {{Ägyptologie TblHighlight}} |colspan="7" align=center style="background:#FFE39B"|'''Dating of Tepi-a Sopdet in Memphis''' |- {{Ägyptologie TblHighlight}} !Year ![[Culmination (astronomy)|Acronychal
Culmination]] ![[Acronychal|Acronychal
Setting]] ![[Heliacal rising|Heliacal
Rising]]The altitude values refer to the end of the respective night hour. Heliacal rising occurs before sunrise, acronychal setting, and acronychal culmination after sunset. !Remarks |- bgcolor="#fffaf0" | style="text-align:center" | 1873 BCE | style="text-align:center" | {{ill|Achet#Dating|lt=26th Achet II|de|26. Achet II|12=draft}}
January 11–12
Altitude 30.3°Actual culmination: 36.5°, approximately 2.75 hours after sunset. | style="text-align:center" | {{ill|Peret#Dating|lt=26th Peret I|de|26. Peret I|12=draft}}
April 11–12
Altitude 10.4°The observation of the setting was only possible towards the end of the decade. | style="text-align:center" | {{ill|Peret#Dating|lt=6th Peret IV|de|6. Peret IV|12=draft}}
June 20–21
Altitude 2.3°On the 10th decade day, the altitude was 9.3°. A heliacal observation during the decade was therefore not possible. | style="text-align:center" | {{ill|Senusret III|de|Sesostris III.|12=draft}} |- bgcolor="#f5f5f5" | style="text-align:center" | 1541 BCE | style="text-align:center" | 19th Peret I
January 14–15 | style="text-align:center" | 19th Peret IV

The entries in the {{ill|Book of Nut|de|Nutbuch|12=draft|12=draft}} regarding decan positions can therefore only refer to the acronychal {{ill|Culmination (astronomy)|de|Kulmination (Astronomie)|12=draft|12=draft}}, as only in this context could Murzim rank before Sirius in the decan lists. During the era of the {{ill|Diagonal star clock|de|Diagonalsternuhren|12=draft|12=draft}}, Murzim reached a culmination altitude of approximately 36.6° in Ancient Egypt. Sirius itself was part of the Ancient Egyptian constellation {{ill|Sopdet (constellation)|de|Sopdet (Sternbild)|12=draft|12=draft}}. {{ill|Alexandra von Lieven|fr|Alexandra von Lieven|de|Alexandra von Lieven|12=draft|12=draft}} notes the decan star "Tepi-a Sopdet" mentioned in the {{ill|Book of Nut|de|Nutbuch|12=draft|12=draft}}, which in the seventh year of {{ill|Senusret III|de|Sesostris III.|12=draft|12=draft}} was listed as the 34th decan with the {{ill|Heliacal rising|de|Heliakisch|12=draft|12=draft}} assigned to the {{ill|Peret#Dating|lt=6th Peret IV|de|6. Peret IV|12=draft|12=draft}} (approximately June 15/21); the {{ill|Culmination (astronomy)|de|Kulmination (Astronomie)|12=draft|12=draft}} for the {{ill|Achet#Dating|lt=26th Achet II|de|26. Achet II|12=draft|12=draft}}, and the acronychal setting for the 26th Peret I. The recorded period of invisibility lasted the canonical 70 days.Alexandra von Lieven: ''Grundriss des Laufes der Sterne''. p. 66. == Dating of Tepi-a Sopdet (Book of Nut System) == {| class="wikitable" style="width: 98%; border: 2px solid gray;" |- {{Ägyptologie TblHighlight}} |colspan="7" align=center style="background:#FFE39B"|'''Dating of Tepi-a Sopdet in Memphis''' |- {{Ägyptologie TblHighlight}} !Year ![[Culmination (astronomy)|Acronychal
Culmination]] ![[Acronychal|Acronychal
Setting]] ![[Heliacal rising|Heliacal
Rising]]The altitude values refer to the end of the respective night hour. Heliacal rising occurs before sunrise, acronychal setting, and acronychal culmination after sunset. !Remarks |- bgcolor="#fffaf0" | style="text-align:center" | 1873 BCE | style="text-align:center" | {{ill|Achet#Dating|lt=26th Achet II|de|26. Achet II|12=draft|12=draft}}
January 11–12
Altitude 30.3°Actual culmination: 36.5°, approximately 2.75 hours after sunset. | style="text-align:center" | {{ill|Peret#Dating|lt=26th Peret I|de|26. Peret I|12=draft|12=draft}}
April 11–12
Altitude 10.4°The observation of the setting was only possible towards the end of the decade. | style="text-align:center" | {{ill|Peret#Dating|lt=6th Peret IV|de|6. Peret IV|12=draft|12=draft}}
June 20–21
Altitude 2.3°On the 10th decade day, the altitude was 9.3°. A heliacal observation during the decade was therefore not possible. | style="text-align:center" | {{ill|Senusret III|de|Sesostris III.|12=draft|12=draft}} |- bgcolor="#f5f5f5" | style="text-align:center" | 1541 BCE | style="text-align:center" | 19th Peret I
January 14–15 | style="text-align:center" | 19th Peret IV
April 14–15 | style="text-align:center" | {{ill|Season of the Harvest|lt=Shemu#Dating|lt=29th Shemu II|de|29. Schemu II|12=draft|12=draft}}
June 23–24 | style="text-align:center" | {{ill|Ebers calendar|de|Ebers-Kalender|12=draft|12=draft}} |- bgcolor="#fffaf0" | style="text-align:center" | 1463 BCE | style="text-align:center" | 8th Peret II
January 15–16 | style="text-align:center" | 8th Shemu I
April 15–16 | style="text-align:center" | 18th Shemu III
June 24–25 | style="text-align:center" | {{ill|Ramesside star clocks|de|Ramessidische Sternuhren|12=draft|12=draft}} |- |} == Literature == * {{Interlanguage link|12=draft|Christian Leitz|fr|Christian Leitz|en|Christian Leitz|de|Christian Leitz}}: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren''. Peeters, Leuven 1995, ISBN 90-6831-669-9, p. 95. * {{ill|Alexandra von Lieven|fr|Alexandra von Lieven|de|Alexandra von Lieven|12=draft|12=draft}}: ''Grundriss des Laufes der Sterne – Das sogenannte Nutbuch''. {{Infobox Hieroglyphen |NAME = D1:D36 M44-X1 N14-N33 |NAME-TRANSKRIPTION = Tepi-a Sopdet
''Tpj-ˁ Spd.t''
''Forerunner of Sopdet'' |BILD1 = Position Beta Cma.png |BILD1-BREITE = 250px |BILD1-BESCHREIBUNG = Constellation “Sopdet” ([[Canis Major]]) }} '''Tepi-a Sopdet''' is the designation of an {{ill|Ancient Egypt|de|Altes Ägypten|12=draft|12=draft}} {{ill|Decade (Ancient Egypt)|de|Dekade (Altägypten)|1=Decan|12=draft|12=draft}}, which comprised only one {{ill|Baktiu|de|Baktiu|12=draft|12=draft}} (decan star). == Background == This decan corresponds to {{ill|Murzim|de|Murzim|12=draft|12=draft}}, the forerunner of {{ill|Sirius|de|Sirius|12=draft|12=draft}}. The traditional {{ill|Arabic language|de|Arabische Sprache|12=draft|12=draft}} name, "Harbinger," likely refers to its position, as it rises before Sirius and heralds the brightest star in the night sky. However, this role does not apply to {{ill|Heliacal rising|de|Heliakisch|12=draft|12=draft}} because Murzim is significantly dimmer than Sirius and requires a {{ill|Altitude|de|Sehungsbogen|12=draft|12=draft}} of at least 13° to become heliacally visible, or approximately 55 minutes before the sun rises. Furthermore, Murzim requires a minimum horizon altitude of 1.7° due to atmospheric refraction. The same conditions apply to {{ill|Acronychal setting|de|Akronychisch|12=draft|12=draft}}. The entries in the {{ill|Book of Nut|de|Nutbuch|12=draft|12=draft}} regarding decan positions can therefore only refer to the acronychal {{ill|Culmination (astronomy)|de|Kulmination (Astronomie)|12=draft|12=draft}}, as only in this context could Murzim rank before Sirius in the decan lists. During the era of the {{ill|Diagonal star clock|de|Diagonalsternuhren|12=draft|12=draft}}, Murzim reached a culmination altitude of approximately 36.6° in Ancient Egypt. Sirius itself was part of the Ancient Egyptian constellation {{ill|Sopdet (constellation)|de|Sopdet (Sternbild)|12=draft|12=draft}}. {{ill|Alexandra von Lieven|fr|Alexandra von Lieven|de|Alexandra von Lieven|12=draft|12=draft}} notes the decan star "Tepi-a Sopdet" mentioned in the {{ill|Book of Nut|de|Nutbuch|12=draft|12=draft}}, which in the seventh year of {{ill|Senusret III|de|Sesostris III.|12=draft|12=draft}} was listed as the 34th decan with the {{ill|Heliacal rising|de|Heliakisch|12=draft|12=draft}} assigned to the {{ill|Peret#Dating|lt=6th Peret IV|de|6. Peret IV|12=draft|12=draft}} (approximately June 15/21); the {{ill|Culmination (astronomy)|de|Kulmination (Astronomie)|12=draft|12=draft}} for the {{ill|Achet#Dating|lt=26th Achet II|de|26. Achet II|12=draft|12=draft}}, and the acronychal setting for the 26th Peret I. The recorded period of invisibility lasted the canonical 70 days.Alexandra von Lieven: ''Grundriss des Laufes der Sterne''. p. 66. == Dating of Tepi-a Sopdet (Book of Nut System) == {| class="wikitable" style="width: 98%; border: 2px solid gray;" |- {{Ägyptologie TblHighlight}} |colspan="7" align=center style="background:#FFE39B"|'''Dating of Tepi-a Sopdet in Memphis''' |- {{Ägyptologie TblHighlight}} !Year ![[Culmination (astronomy)|Acronychal

Heliacal rising occurs before sunrise, acronychal setting, and acronychal culmination after sunset. !Remarks |- bgcolor="#fffaf0" | style="text-align:center" | 1873 BCE | style="text-align:center" | {{ill|Achet#Dating|lt=26th Achet II|de|26. April 11–12
Altitude 10.4°The observation of the setting was only possible towards the end of the decade. | style="text-align:center" | {{ill|Peret#Dating|lt=6th Peret IV|de|6. Peret IV|12=draft|12=draft}}
June 20–21
Altitude 2.3°On the 10th decade day, the altitude was 9.3°. A heliacal observation during the decade was therefore not possible. | style="text-align:center" | {{ill|Senusret III|de|Sesostris III.|12=draft|12=draft}} |- bgcolor="#f5f5f5" | style="text-align:center" | 1541 BCE | style="text-align:center" | 19th Peret I

''Forerunner of Sopdet'' |BILD1 = Position Beta Cma.png |BILD1-BREITE = 250px |BILD1-BESCHREIBUNG = Constellation “Sopdet” ([[Canis Major]]) }} '''Tepi-a Sopdet''' is the designation of an {{ill|Ancient Egypt|de|Altes Ägypten|12=draft|12=draft}} {{ill|Decade (Ancient Egypt)|de|Dekade (Altägypten)|1=Decan|12=draft|12=draft}}, which comprised only one {{ill|Baktiu|de|Baktiu|12=draft|12=draft}} (decan star). == Background == This decan corresponds to {{ill|Murzim|de|Murzim|12=draft|12=draft}}, the forerunner of {{ill|Sirius|de|Sirius|12=draft|12=draft}}. The traditional {{ill|Arabic language|de|Arabische Sprache|12=draft|12=draft}} name, "Harbinger," likely refers to its position, as it rises before Sirius and heralds the brightest star in the night sky. However, this role does not apply to {{ill|Heliacal rising|de|Heliakisch|12=draft|12=draft}} because Murzim is significantly dimmer than Sirius and requires a {{ill|Altitude|de|Sehungsbogen|12=draft|12=draft}} of at least 13° to become heliacally visible, or approximately 55 minutes before the sun rises. Furthermore, Murzim requires a minimum horizon altitude of 1.7° due to atmospheric refraction. The same conditions apply to {{ill|Acronychal setting|de|Akronychisch|12=draft|12=draft}}. The entries in the {{ill|Book of Nut|de|Nutbuch|12=draft|12=draft}} regarding decan positions can therefore only refer to the acronychal {{ill|Culmination (astronomy)|de|Kulmination (Astronomie)|12=draft|12=draft}}, as only in this context could Murzim rank before Sirius in the decan lists. During the era of the {{ill|Diagonal star clock|de|Diagonalsternuhren|12=draft|12=draft}}, Murzim reached a culmination altitude of approximately 36.6° in Ancient Egypt. Sirius itself was part of the Ancient Egyptian constellation {{ill|Sopdet (constellation)|de|Sopdet (Sternbild)|12=draft|12=draft}}. {{ill|Alexandra von Lieven|fr|Alexandra von Lieven|de|Alexandra von Lieven|12=draft|12=draft}} notes the decan star "Tepi-a Sopdet" mentioned in the {{ill|Book of Nut|de|Nutbuch|12=draft|12=draft}}, which in the seventh year of {{ill|Senusret III|de|Sesostris III.|12=draft|12=draft}} was listed as the 34th decan with the {{ill|Heliacal rising|de|Heliakisch|12=draft|12=draft}} assigned to the {{ill|Peret#Dating|lt=6th Peret IV|de|6. Peret IV|12=draft|12=draft}} (approximately June 15/21); the {{ill|Culmination (astronomy)|de|Kulmination (Astronomie)|12=draft|12=draft}} for the {{ill|Achet#Dating|lt=26th Achet II|de|26. Achet II|12=draft|12=draft}}, and the acronychal setting for the 26th Peret I. The recorded period of invisibility lasted the canonical 70 days.Alexandra von Lieven: ''Grundriss des Laufes der Sterne''. p. 66. == Dating of Tepi-a Sopdet (Book of Nut System) == {| class="wikitable" style="width: 98%; border: 2px solid gray;" |- {{Ägyptologie TblHighlight}} |colspan="7" align=center style="background:#FFE39B"|'''Dating of Tepi-a Sopdet in Memphis''' |- {{Ägyptologie TblHighlight}} !Year ![[Culmination (astronomy)|Acronychal
Culmination]] ![[Acronychal|Acronychal
Setting]] ![[Heliacal rising|Heliacal
Rising]]The altitude values refer to the end of the respective night hour. Heliacal rising occurs before sunrise, acronychal setting, and acronychal culmination after sunset. !Remarks |- bgcolor="#fffaf0" | style="text-align:center" | 1873 BCE | style="text-align:center" | {{ill|Achet#Dating|lt=26th Achet II|de|26. Achet II|12=draft|12=draft}}
January 11–12
Altitude 30.3°Actual culmination: 36.5°, approximately 2.75 hours after sunset. | style="text-align:center" | {{ill|Peret#Dating|lt=26th Peret I|de|26. Peret I|12=draft|12=draft}}
April 11–12
Altitude 10.4°The observation of the setting was only possible towards the end of the decade. | style="text-align:center" | {{ill|Peret#Dating|lt=6th Peret IV|de|6. Peret IV|12=draft|12=draft}}
June 20–21
Altitude 2.3°On the 10th decade day, the altitude was 9.3°. A heliacal observation during the decade was therefore not possible. | style="text-align:center" | {{ill|Senusret III|de|Sesostris III.|12=draft|12=draft}} |- bgcolor="#f5f5f5" | style="text-align:center" | 1541 BCE | style="text-align:center" | 19th Peret I
January 14–15 | style="text-align:center" | 19th Peret IV
April 14–15 | style="text-align:center" | {{ill|Season of the Harvest|lt=Shemu#Dating|lt=29th Shemu II|de|29. Schemu II|12=draft|12=draft}}
June 23–24 | style="text-align:center" | {{ill|Ebers calendar|de|Ebers-Kalender|12=draft|12=draft}} |- bgcolor="#fffaf0" | style="text-align:center" | 1463 BCE | style="text-align:center" | 8th Peret II
January 15–16 | style="text-align:center" | 8th Shemu I
April 15–16 | style="text-align:center" | 18th Shemu III
June 24–25 | style="text-align:center" | {{ill|Ramesside star clocks|de|Ramessidische Sternuhren|12=draft|12=draft}} |- |} == Literature == * {{Interlanguage link|12=draft|Christian Leitz|fr|Christian Leitz|en|Christian Leitz|de|Christian Leitz}}: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren''. Peeters, Leuven 1995, ISBN 90-6831-669-9, p. 95. * {{ill|Alexandra von Lieven|fr|Alexandra von Lieven|de|Alexandra von Lieven|12=draft|12=draft}}: ''Grundriss des Laufes der Sterne – Das sogenannte Nutbuch''. The Carsten Niebuhr Institute of Ancient Eastern Studies (et al.), Copenhagen 2007, ISBN 978-87-635-0406-5, pp. 62 and 398. == References == {{draft categories| [[Category:Decan star (Ancient Egypt)]] [[Category:WikiProject Ancient Egypt]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:Tepi-a Sopdet]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:pages which might possibly be incomplete but are most likely complete]] [[Category:I think I cleared all the incomplete or blank pages earlier]] }} [[en:draft:Tepi-a Sopdet]] [[de:Tepi-a Sopdet]] [[sn:Tepi-a-Sopdet]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Tepi-a-Sopdet}}

{{Infobox Hieroglyphen |NAME = D1:D36 M44-X1 N14-N33 |NAME-TRANSKRIPTION = Tepi-a Sopdet
''Tpj-ˁ Spd.t''
''Forerunner of Sopdet'' |BILD1 = Position Beta Cma.png |BILD1-BREITE = 250px |BILD1-BESCHREIBUNG = Constellation “Sopdet” ([[Canis Major]]) }} '''Tepi-a Sopdet''' is the designation of an {{ill|Ancient Egypt|de|Altes Ägypten|12=draft|12=draft}} {{ill|Decade (Ancient Egypt)|de|Dekade (Altägypten)|1=Decan|12=draft|12=draft}}, which comprised only one {{ill|Baktiu|de|Baktiu|12=draft|12=draft}} (decan star). == Background == This decan corresponds to {{ill|Murzim|de|Murzim|12=draft|12=draft}}, the forerunner of {{ill|Sirius|de|Sirius|12=draft|12=draft}}. The traditional {{ill|Arabic language|de|Arabische Sprache|12=draft|12=draft}} name, "Harbinger," likely refers to its position, as it rises before Sirius and heralds the brightest star in the night sky. However, this role does not apply to {{ill|Heliacal rising|de|Heliakisch|12=draft|12=draft}} because Murzim is significantly dimmer than Sirius and requires a {{ill|Altitude|de|Sehungsbogen|12=draft|12=draft}} of at least 13° to become heliacally visible, or approximately 55 minutes before the sun rises. Furthermore, Murzim requires a minimum horizon altitude of 1.7° due to atmospheric refraction. The same conditions apply to {{ill|Acronychal setting|de|Akronychisch|12=draft|12=draft}}. The entries in the {{ill|Book of Nut|de|Nutbuch|12=draft|12=draft}} regarding decan positions can therefore only refer to the acronychal {{ill|Culmination (astronomy)|de|Kulmination (Astronomie)|12=draft|12=draft}}, as only in this context could Murzim rank before Sirius in the decan lists. During the era of the {{ill|Diagonal star clock|de|Diagonalsternuhren|12=draft|12=draft}}, Murzim reached a culmination altitude of approximately 36.6° in Ancient Egypt. Sirius itself was part of the Ancient Egyptian constellation {{ill|Sopdet (constellation)|de|Sopdet (Sternbild)|12=draft|12=draft}}. {{ill|Alexandra von Lieven|fr|Alexandra von Lieven|de|Alexandra von Lieven|12=draft|12=draft}} notes the decan star "Tepi-a Sopdet" mentioned in the {{ill|Book of Nut|de|Nutbuch|12=draft|12=draft}}, which in the seventh year of {{ill|Senusret III|de|Sesostris III.|12=draft|12=draft}} was listed as the 34th decan with the {{ill|Heliacal rising|de|Heliakisch|12=draft|12=draft}} assigned to the {{ill|Peret#Dating|lt=6th Peret IV|de|6. Peret IV|12=draft|12=draft}} (approximately June 15/21); the {{ill|Culmination (astronomy)|de|Kulmination (Astronomie)|12=draft|12=draft}} for the {{ill|Achet#Dating|lt=26th Achet II|de|26. Achet II|12=draft|12=draft}}, and the acronychal setting for the 26th Peret I. The recorded period of invisibility lasted the canonical 70 days.Alexandra von Lieven: ''Grundriss des Laufes der Sterne''. p. 66. == Dating of Tepi-a Sopdet (Book of Nut System) == {| class="wikitable" style="width: 98%; border: 2px solid gray;" |- {{Ägyptologie TblHighlight}} |colspan="7" align=center style="background:#FFE39B"|'''Dating of Tepi-a Sopdet in Memphis''' |- {{Ägyptologie TblHighlight}} !Year ![[Culmination (astronomy)|Acronychal
Culmination]] ![[Acronychal|Acronychal
Setting]] ![[Heliacal rising|Heliacal
Rising]]The altitude values refer to the end of the respective night hour. Heliacal rising occurs before sunrise, acronychal setting, and acronychal culmination after sunset. !Remarks |- bgcolor="#fffaf0" | style="text-align:center" | 1873 BCE | style="text-align:center" | {{ill|Achet#Dating|lt=26th Achet II|de|26. Achet II|12=draft|12=draft}}
January 11–12
Altitude 30.3°Actual culmination: 36.5°, approximately 2.75 hours after sunset. | style="text-align:center" | {{ill|Peret#Dating|lt=26th Peret I|de|26. Peret I|12=draft|12=draft}}
April 11–12
Altitude 10.4°The observation of the setting was only possible towards the end of the decade. | style="text-align:center" | {{ill|Peret#Dating|lt=6th Peret IV|de|6. Peret IV|12=draft|12=draft}}
June 20–21
Altitude 2.3°On the 10th decade day, the altitude was 9.3°. A heliacal observation during the decade was therefore not possible. | style="text-align:center" | {{ill|Senusret III|de|Sesostris III.|12=draft|12=draft}} |- bgcolor="#f5f5f5" | style="text-align:center" | 1541 BCE | style="text-align:center" | 19th Peret I
January 14–15 | style="text-align:center" | 19th Peret IV
April 14–15 | style="text-align:center" | {{ill|Season of the Harvest|lt=Shemu#Dating|lt=29th Shemu II|de|29. Schemu II|12=draft|12=draft}}
June 23–24 | style="text-align:center" | {{ill|Ebers calendar|de|Ebers-Kalender|12=draft|12=draft}} |- bgcolor="#fffaf0" | style="text-align:center" | 1463 BCE | style="text-align:center" | 8th Peret II
January 15–16 | style="text-align:center" | 8th Shemu I
April 15–16 | style="text-align:center" | 18th Shemu III
June 24–25 | style="text-align:center" | {{ill|Ramesside star clocks|de|Ramessidische Sternuhren|12=draft|12=draft}} |- |} == Literature == * {{Interlanguage link|12=draft|Christian Leitz|fr|Christian Leitz|en|Christian Leitz|de|Christian Leitz}}: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren''. Peeters, Leuven 1995, ISBN 90-6831-669-9, p. 95. * {{ill|Alexandra von Lieven|fr|Alexandra von Lieven|de|Alexandra von Lieven|12=draft|12=draft}}: ''Grundriss des Laufes der Sterne – Das sogenannte Nutbuch''. The Carsten Niebuhr Institute of Ancient Eastern Studies (et al.), Copenhagen 2007, ISBN 978-87-635-0406-5, pp. 62 and 398. == References == {{draft categories| [[Category:Decan star (Ancient Egypt)]] [[Category:WikiProject Ancient Egypt]] [[Category:Tepi-a Sopdet]] [[Category:11]] }} [[en:draft:Tepi-a Sopdet]] [[de:Tepi-a Sopdet]] [[sn:Tepi-a-Sopdet]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Tepi-a-Sopdet}}

''Forerunner of Sopdet'' |BILD1 = Position Beta Cma.png |BILD1-BREITE = 250px |BILD1-BESCHREIBUNG = Constellation “Sopdet” ([[Canis Major]]) }} '''Tepi-a Sopdet''' is the designation of an {{ill|Ancient Egypt|de|Altes Ägypten|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}} {{ill|Decade (Ancient Egypt)|de|Dekade (Altägypten)|1=Decan|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}, which comprised only one {{ill|Baktiu|de|Baktiu|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}} (decan star). == Background == This decan corresponds to {{ill|Murzim|de|Murzim|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}, the forerunner of {{ill|Sirius|de|Sirius|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}. The traditional {{ill|Arabic language|de|Arabische Sprache|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}} name, "Harbinger," likely refers to its position, as it rises before Sirius and heralds the brightest star in the night sky. However, this role does not apply to {{ill|Heliacal rising|de|Heliakisch|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}} because Murzim is significantly dimmer than Sirius and requires a {{ill|Altitude|de|Sehungsbogen|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}} of at least 13° to become heliacally visible, or approximately 55 minutes before the sun rises. Furthermore, Murzim requires a minimum horizon altitude of 1.7° due to atmospheric refraction. The same conditions apply to {{ill|Acronychal setting|de|Akronychisch|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}. The entries in the {{ill|Book of Nut|de|Nutbuch|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}} regarding decan positions can therefore only refer to the acronychal {{ill|Culmination (astronomy)|de|Kulmination (Astronomie)|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}, as only in this context could Murzim rank before Sirius in the decan lists. During the era of the {{ill|Diagonal star clock|de|Diagonalsternuhren|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}, Murzim reached a culmination altitude of approximately 36.6° in Ancient Egypt. Sirius itself was part of the Ancient Egyptian constellation {{ill|Sopdet (constellation)|de|Sopdet (Sternbild)|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}. {{ill|Alexandra von Lieven|fr|Alexandra von Lieven|de|Alexandra von Lieven|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}} notes the decan star "Tepi-a Sopdet" mentioned in the {{ill|Book of Nut|de|Nutbuch|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}, which in the seventh year of {{ill|Senusret III|de|Sesostris III.|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}} was listed as the 34th decan with the {{ill|Heliacal rising|de|Heliakisch|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}} assigned to the {{ill|Peret#Dating|lt=6th Peret IV|de|6. Peret IV|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}} (approximately June 15/21); the {{ill|Culmination (astronomy)|de|Kulmination (Astronomie)|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}} for the {{ill|Achet#Dating|lt=26th Achet II|de|26. Achet II|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}, and the acronychal setting for the 26th Peret I. The recorded period of invisibility lasted the canonical 70 days.Alexandra von Lieven: ''Grundriss des Laufes der Sterne''. p. 66. == Dating of Tepi-a Sopdet (Book of Nut System) == {| class="wikitable" style="width: 98%; border: 2px solid gray;" |- {{Ägyptologie TblHighlight}} |colspan="7" align=center style="background:#FFE39B"|'''Dating of Tepi-a Sopdet in Memphis''' |- {{Ägyptologie TblHighlight}} !Year ![[Culmination (astronomy)|Acronychal
