June 24–25 | style="text-align:center" | {{ill|Ramesside star clocks|de|Ramessidische Sternuhren|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}} |- |} == Literature == * {{Interlanguage link|12=draft|Christian Leitz|fr|Christian Leitz|en|Christian Leitz|de|Christian Leitz}}: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren''. Peeters, Leuven 1995, ISBN 90-6831-669-9, p. 95. * {{ill|Alexandra von Lieven|fr|Alexandra von Lieven|de|Alexandra von Lieven|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}: ''Grundriss des Laufes der Sterne – Das sogenannte Nutbuch''. The Carsten Niebuhr Institute of Ancient Eastern Studies (et al.), Copenhagen 2007, ISBN 978-87-635-0406-5, pp. 62 and 398. == References == {{draft categories| }} {{draft categories| [[Category:Pages without meta categories]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Tepi-a-Sopdet]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:Decan star (Ancient Egypt)]] [[Category:WikiProject Ancient Egypt]] [[Category:Tepi-a Sopdet]] [[Category:11]] }} [[en:draft:Tepi-a Sopdet]] [[de:Tepi-a Sopdet]] [[sn:Tepi-a-Sopdet]] {{citation needed|Tepi-a-Sopdet}}

revision.comment: Text replacement - "rank" to "Rank"
revision.text:
{{Infobox Hieroglyphen |NAME = D1:D36 M44-X1 N14-N33 |NAME-TRANSKRIPTION = Tepi-a Sopdet
''Tpj-ˁ Spd.t''
''Forerunner of Sopdet'' |BILD1 = Position Beta Cma.png |BILD1-BREITE = 250px |BILD1-BESCHREIBUNG = Constellation “Sopdet” ([[Canis Major]]) }} '''Tepi-a Sopdet''' is the designation of an {{ill|Ancient Egypt|de|Altes Ägypten|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}} {{ill|Decade (Ancient Egypt)|de|Dekade (Altägypten)|1=Decan|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}, which comprised only one {{ill|Baktiu|de|Baktiu|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}} (decan star). == Background == This decan corresponds to {{ill|Murzim|de|Murzim|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}, the forerunner of {{ill|Sirius|de|Sirius|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}. The traditional {{ill|Arabic language|de|Arabische Sprache|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}} name, "Harbinger," likely refers to its position, as it rises before Sirius and heralds the brightest star in the night sky. However, this role does not apply to {{ill|Heliacal rising|de|Heliakisch|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}} because Murzim is significantly dimmer than Sirius and requires a {{ill|Altitude|de|Sehungsbogen|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}} of at least 13° to become heliacally visible, or approximately 55 minutes before the sun rises. Furthermore, Murzim requires a minimum horizon altitude of 1.7° due to atmospheric refraction. The same conditions apply to {{ill|Acronychal setting|de|Akronychisch|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}. The entries in the {{ill|Book of Nut|de|Nutbuch|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}} regarding decan positions can therefore only refer to the acronychal {{ill|Culmination (astronomy)|de|Kulmination (Astronomie)|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}, as only in this context could Murzim Rank before Sirius in the decan lists. During the era of the {{ill|Diagonal star clock|de|Diagonalsternuhren|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}, Murzim reached a culmination altitude of approximately 36.6° in Ancient Egypt. Sirius itself was part of the Ancient Egyptian constellation {{ill|Sopdet (constellation)|de|Sopdet (Sternbild)|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}. {{ill|Alexandra von Lieven|fr|Alexandra von Lieven|de|Alexandra von Lieven|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}} notes the decan star "Tepi-a Sopdet" mentioned in the {{ill|Book of Nut|de|Nutbuch|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}, which in the seventh year of {{ill|Senusret III|de|Sesostris III.|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}} was listed as the 34th decan with the {{ill|Heliacal rising|de|Heliakisch|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}} assigned to the {{ill|Peret#Dating|lt=6th Peret IV|de|6. Peret IV|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}} (approximately June 15/21); the {{ill|Culmination (astronomy)|de|Kulmination (Astronomie)|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}} for the {{ill|Achet#Dating|lt=26th Achet II|de|26. Achet II|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}, and the acronychal setting for the 26th Peret I. The recorded period of invisibility lasted the canonical 70 days.Alexandra von Lieven: ''Grundriss des Laufes der Sterne''. p. 66. == Dating of Tepi-a Sopdet (Book of Nut System) == {| class="wikitable" style="width: 98%; border: 2px solid gray;" |- {{Ägyptologie TblHighlight}} |colspan="7" align=center style="background:#FFE39B"|'''Dating of Tepi-a Sopdet in Memphis''' |- {{Ägyptologie TblHighlight}} !Year ![[Culmination (astronomy)|Acronychal
Culmination]] ![[Acronychal|Acronychal
Setting]] ![[Heliacal rising|Heliacal
Rising]]The altitude values refer to the end of the respective night hour. Heliacal rising occurs before sunrise, acronychal setting, and acronychal culmination after sunset. !Remarks |- bgcolor="#fffaf0" | style="text-align:center" | 1873 BCE | style="text-align:center" | {{ill|Achet#Dating|lt=26th Achet II|de|26. Achet II|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}
January 11–12
Altitude 30.3°Actual culmination: 36.5°, approximately 2.75 hours after sunset. | style="text-align:center" | {{ill|Peret#Dating|lt=26th Peret I|de|26. Peret I|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}
April 11–12
Altitude 10.4°The observation of the setting was only possible towards the end of the decade. | style="text-align:center" | {{ill|Peret#Dating|lt=6th Peret IV|de|6. Peret IV|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}
June 20–21
Altitude 2.3°On the 10th decade day, the altitude was 9.3°. A heliacal observation during the decade was therefore not possible. | style="text-align:center" | {{ill|Senusret III|de|Sesostris III.|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}} |- bgcolor="#f5f5f5" | style="text-align:center" | 1541 BCE | style="text-align:center" | 19th Peret I
January 14–15 | style="text-align:center" | 19th Peret IV
April 14–15 | style="text-align:center" | {{ill|Season of the Harvest|lt=Shemu#Dating|lt=29th Shemu II|de|29. Schemu II|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}
June 23–24 | style="text-align:center" | {{ill|Ebers calendar|de|Ebers-Kalender|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}} |- bgcolor="#fffaf0" | style="text-align:center" | 1463 BCE | style="text-align:center" | 8th Peret II
January 15–16 | style="text-align:center" | 8th Shemu I
April 15–16 | style="text-align:center" | 18th Shemu III
June 24–25 | style="text-align:center" | {{ill|Ramesside star clocks|de|Ramessidische Sternuhren|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}} |- |} == Literature == * {{Interlanguage link|12=draft|Christian Leitz|fr|Christian Leitz|en|Christian Leitz|de|Christian Leitz}}: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren''. Peeters, Leuven 1995, ISBN 90-6831-669-9, p. 95. * {{ill|Alexandra von Lieven|fr|Alexandra von Lieven|de|Alexandra von Lieven|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}: ''Grundriss des Laufes der Sterne – Das sogenannte Nutbuch''. The Carsten Niebuhr Institute of Ancient Eastern Studies (et al.), Copenhagen 2007, ISBN 978-87-635-0406-5, pp. 62 and 398. == References == {{draft categories| }} {{draft categories| [[Category:Pages without meta categories]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Tepi-a-Sopdet]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:Decan star (Ancient Egypt)]] [[Category:WikiProject Ancient Egypt]] [[Category:Tepi-a Sopdet]] [[Category:11]] }} [[en:draft:Tepi-a Sopdet]] [[de:Tepi-a Sopdet]] [[sn:Tepi-a-Sopdet]] {{citation needed|Tepi-a-Sopdet}}

revision.comment: Text replacement - "|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft" to "|12=draft"
revision.text:
{{Infobox Hieroglyphen |NAME = D1:D36 M44-X1 N14-N33 |NAME-TRANSKRIPTION = Tepi-a Sopdet
''Tpj-ˁ Spd.t''
''Forerunner of Sopdet'' |BILD1 = Position Beta Cma.png |BILD1-BREITE = 250px |BILD1-BESCHREIBUNG = Constellation “Sopdet” ([[Canis Major]]) }} '''Tepi-a Sopdet''' is the designation of an {{ill|Ancient Egypt|de|Altes Ägypten|12=draft}} {{ill|Decade (Ancient Egypt)|de|Dekade (Altägypten)|1=Decan|12=draft}}, which comprised only one {{ill|Baktiu|de|Baktiu|12=draft}} (decan star). == Background == This decan corresponds to {{ill|Murzim|de|Murzim|12=draft}}, the forerunner of {{ill|Sirius|de|Sirius|12=draft}}. The traditional {{ill|Arabic language|de|Arabische Sprache|12=draft}} name, "Harbinger," likely refers to its position, as it rises before Sirius and heralds the brightest star in the night sky. However, this role does not apply to {{ill|Heliacal rising|de|Heliakisch|12=draft}} because Murzim is significantly dimmer than Sirius and requires a {{ill|Altitude|de|Sehungsbogen|12=draft}} of at least 13° to become heliacally visible, or approximately 55 minutes before the sun rises. Furthermore, Murzim requires a minimum horizon altitude of 1.7° due to atmospheric refraction. The same conditions apply to {{ill|Acronychal setting|de|Akronychisch|1=Acronychic|12=draft}}. The entries in the {{ill|Book of Nut|de|Nutbuch|12=draft}} regarding decan positions can therefore only refer to the acronychal {{ill|Culmination (astronomy)|de|Kulmination (Astronomie)|12=draft}}, as only in this context could Murzim Rank before Sirius in the decan lists. During the era of the {{ill|Diagonal star clock|de|Diagonalsternuhren|12=draft}}, Murzim reached a culmination altitude of approximately 36.6° in Ancient Egypt. Sirius itself was part of the Ancient Egyptian constellation {{ill|Sopdet (constellation)|de|Sopdet (Sternbild)|12=draft}}. {{ill|Alexandra von Lieven|fr|Alexandra von Lieven|de|Alexandra von Lieven|12=draft}} notes the decan star "Tepi-a Sopdet" mentioned in the {{ill|Book of Nut|de|Nutbuch|12=draft}}, which in the seventh year of {{ill|Senusret III|de|Sesostris III.|12=draft}} was listed as the 34th decan with the {{ill|Heliacal rising|de|Heliakisch|12=draft}} assigned to the {{ill|Peret#Dating|lt=6th Peret IV|de|6. Peret IV|12=draft}} (approximately June 15/21); the {{ill|Culmination (astronomy)|de|Kulmination (Astronomie)|12=draft}} for the {{ill|Achet#Dating|lt=26th Achet II|de|26. Achet II|12=draft}}, and the acronychal setting for the 26th Peret I. The recorded period of invisibility lasted the canonical 70 days.Alexandra von Lieven: ''Grundriss des Laufes der Sterne''. p. 66. == Dating of Tepi-a Sopdet (Book of Nut System) == {| class="wikitable" style="width: 98%; border: 2px solid gray;" |- {{Ägyptologie TblHighlight}} |colspan="7" align=center style="background:#FFE39B"|'''Dating of Tepi-a Sopdet in Memphis''' |- {{Ägyptologie TblHighlight}} !Year ![[Culmination (astronomy)|Acronychal
Culmination]] ![[Acronychal|Acronychal
Setting]] ![[Heliacal rising|Heliacal
Rising]]The altitude values refer to the end of the respective night hour. Heliacal rising occurs before sunrise, acronychal setting, and acronychal culmination after sunset. !Remarks |- bgcolor="#fffaf0" | style="text-align:center" | 1873 BCE | style="text-align:center" | {{ill|Achet#Dating|lt=26th Achet II|de|26. Achet II|12=draft}}
January 11–12
Altitude 30.3°Actual culmination: 36.5°, approximately 2.75 hours after sunset. | style="text-align:center" | {{ill|Peret#Dating|lt=26th Peret I|de|26. Peret I|12=draft}}
April 11–12
Altitude 10.4°The observation of the setting was only possible towards the end of the decade. | style="text-align:center" | {{ill|Peret#Dating|lt=6th Peret IV|de|6. Peret IV|12=draft}}
June 20–21
Altitude 2.3°On the 10th decade day, the altitude was 9.3°. A heliacal observation during the decade was therefore not possible. | style="text-align:center" | {{ill|Senusret III|de|Sesostris III.|12=draft}} |- bgcolor="#f5f5f5" | style="text-align:center" | 1541 BCE | style="text-align:center" | 19th Peret I
January 14–15 | style="text-align:center" | 19th Peret IV
April 14–15 | style="text-align:center" | {{ill|Season of the Harvest|lt=Shemu#Dating|lt=29th Shemu II|de|29. Schemu II|12=draft}}
June 23–24 | style="text-align:center" | {{ill|Ebers calendar|de|Ebers-Kalender|12=draft}} |- bgcolor="#fffaf0" | style="text-align:center" | 1463 BCE | style="text-align:center" | 8th Peret II
January 15–16 | style="text-align:center" | 8th Shemu I
April 15–16 | style="text-align:center" | 18th Shemu III
June 24–25 | style="text-align:center" | {{ill|Ramesside star clocks|de|Ramessidische Sternuhren|12=draft}} |- |} == Literature == * {{ill|Christian Leitz|fr|Christian Leitz|en|Christian Leitz|de|Christian Leitz}}: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren''. Peeters, Leuven 1995, ISBN 90-6831-669-9, p. 95. * {{ill|Alexandra von Lieven|fr|Alexandra von Lieven|de|Alexandra von Lieven|12=draft}}: ''Grundriss des Laufes der Sterne – Das sogenannte Nutbuch''. The Carsten Niebuhr Institute of Ancient Eastern Studies (et al.), Copenhagen 2007, ISBN 978-87-635-0406-5, pp. 62 and 398. == References == {{draft categories| }} {{draft categories| [[Category:Pages without meta categories]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Tepi-a-Sopdet]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:Decan star (Ancient Egypt)]] [[Category:WikiProject Ancient Egypt]] [[Category:Tepi-a Sopdet]] [[Category:11]] }} [[en:draft:Tepi-a Sopdet]] [[de:Tepi-a Sopdet]] [[sn:Tepi-a-Sopdet]] {{citation needed|Tepi-a-Sopdet}}

Altitude 10.4°The observation of the setting was only possible towards the end of the decade. | style="text-align:center" | {{ill|Peret#Dating|lt=6th Peret IV|de|6. Peret IV|qq=draft}}
June 20–21
Altitude 2.3°On the 10th decade day, the altitude was 9.3°. A heliacal observation during the decade was therefore not possible. | style="text-align:center" | {{ill|Senusret III|de|Sesostris III.|qq=draft}} |- bgcolor="#f5f5f5" | style="text-align:center" | 1541 BCE | style="text-align:center" | 19th Peret I

revision.text:
{{Infobox Hieroglyphen |NAME = D1:D36 M44-X1 N14-N33 |NAME-TRANSKRIPTION = Tepi-a Sopdet
''Tpj-ˁ Spd.t''
''Forerunner of Sopdet'' |BILD1 = Position Beta Cma.png |BILD1-BREITE = 250px |BILD1-BESCHREIBUNG = Constellation “Sopdet” ([[Canis Major]]) }} '''Tepi-a Sopdet''' is the designation of an {{ill|Ancient Egypt|de|Altes Ägypten|qq=draft}} {{ill|Decade (Ancient Egypt)|de|Dekade (Altägypten)|1=Decan|qq=draft}}, which comprised only one {{ill|Baktiu|de|Baktiu|qq=draft}} (decan star). == Background == This decan corresponds to {{ill|Murzim|de|Murzim|qq=draft}}, the forerunner of {{ill|Sirius|de|Sirius|qq=draft}}. The traditional {{ill|Arabic language|de|Arabische Sprache|qq=draft}} name, "Harbinger," likely refers to its position, as it rises before Sirius and heralds the brightest star in the night sky. However, this role does not apply to {{ill|Heliacal rising|de|Heliakisch|qq=draft}} because Murzim is significantly dimmer than Sirius and requires a {{ill|Altitude|de|Sehungsbogen|qq=draft}} of at least 13° to become heliacally visible, or approximately 55 minutes before the sun rises. Furthermore, Murzim requires a minimum horizon altitude of 1.7° due to atmospheric refraction. The same conditions apply to {{ill|Acronychal setting|de|Akronychisch|1=Acronychic|qq=draft}}. The entries in the {{ill|Book of Nut|de|Nutbuch|qq=draft}} regarding decan positions can therefore only refer to the acronychal {{ill|Culmination (astronomy)|de|Kulmination (Astronomie)|qq=draft}}, as only in this context could Murzim Rank before Sirius in the decan lists. During the era of the {{ill|Diagonal star clock|de|Diagonalsternuhren|qq=draft}}, Murzim reached a culmination altitude of approximately 36.6° in Ancient Egypt. Sirius itself was part of the Ancient Egyptian constellation {{ill|Sopdet (constellation)|de|Sopdet (Sternbild)|qq=draft}}. {{ill|Alexandra von Lieven|fr|Alexandra von Lieven|de|Alexandra von Lieven|qq=draft}} notes the decan star "Tepi-a Sopdet" mentioned in the {{ill|Book of Nut|de|Nutbuch|qq=draft}}, which in the seventh year of {{ill|Senusret III|de|Sesostris III.|qq=draft}} was listed as the 34th decan with the {{ill|Heliacal rising|de|Heliakisch|qq=draft}} assigned to the {{ill|Peret#Dating|lt=6th Peret IV|de|6. Peret IV|qq=draft}} (approximately June 15/21); the {{ill|Culmination (astronomy)|de|Kulmination (Astronomie)|qq=draft}} for the {{ill|Achet#Dating|lt=26th Achet II|de|26. Achet II|qq=draft}}, and the acronychal setting for the 26th Peret I. The recorded period of invisibility lasted the canonical 70 days.Alexandra von Lieven: ''Grundriss des Laufes der Sterne''. p. 66. == Dating of Tepi-a Sopdet (Book of Nut System) == {| class="wikitable" style="width: 98%; border: 2px solid gray;" |- {{Ägyptologie TblHighlight}} |colspan="7" align=center style="background:#FFE39B"|'''Dating of Tepi-a Sopdet in Memphis''' |- {{Ägyptologie TblHighlight}} !Year ![[Culmination (astronomy)|Acronychal
Culmination]] ![[Acronychal|Acronychal
Setting]] ![[Heliacal rising|Heliacal
Rising]]The altitude values refer to the end of the respective night hour. Heliacal rising occurs before sunrise, acronychal setting, and acronychal culmination after sunset. !Remarks |- bgcolor="#fffaf0" | style="text-align:center" | 1873 BCE | style="text-align:center" | {{ill|Achet#Dating|lt=26th Achet II|de|26. Achet II|qq=draft}}
January 11–12
Altitude 30.3°Actual culmination: 36.5°, approximately 2.75 hours after sunset. | style="text-align:center" | {{ill|Peret#Dating|lt=26th Peret I|de|26. Peret I|qq=draft}}
April 11–12
Altitude 10.4°The observation of the setting was only possible towards the end of the decade. | style="text-align:center" | {{ill|Peret#Dating|lt=6th Peret IV|de|6. Peret IV|qq=draft}}
June 20–21
Altitude 2.3°On the 10th decade day, the altitude was 9.3°. A heliacal observation during the decade was therefore not possible. | style="text-align:center" | {{ill|Senusret III|de|Sesostris III.|qq=draft}} |- bgcolor="#f5f5f5" | style="text-align:center" | 1541 BCE | style="text-align:center" | 19th Peret I
January 14–15 | style="text-align:center" | 19th Peret IV
April 14–15 | style="text-align:center" | {{ill|Season of the Harvest|lt=Shemu#Dating|lt=29th Shemu II|de|29. Schemu II|qq=draft}}
June 23–24 | style="text-align:center" | {{ill|Ebers calendar|de|Ebers-Kalender|qq=draft}} |- bgcolor="#fffaf0" | style="text-align:center" | 1463 BCE | style="text-align:center" | 8th Peret II
January 15–16 | style="text-align:center" | 8th Shemu I
April 15–16 | style="text-align:center" | 18th Shemu III
June 24–25 | style="text-align:center" | {{ill|Ramesside star clocks|de|Ramessidische Sternuhren|qq=draft}} |- |} == Literature == * {{ill|Christian Leitz|fr|Christian Leitz|en|Christian Leitz|de|Christian Leitz}}: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren''. Peeters, Leuven 1995, ISBN 90-6831-669-9, p. 95. * {{ill|Alexandra von Lieven|fr|Alexandra von Lieven|de|Alexandra von Lieven|qq=draft}}: ''Grundriss des Laufes der Sterne – Das sogenannte Nutbuch''. The Carsten Niebuhr Institute of Ancient Eastern Studies (et al.), Copenhagen 2007, ISBN 978-87-635-0406-5, pp. 62 and 398. == References == {{draft categories| }} {{draft categories| [[Category:Pages without meta categories]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Tepi-a-Sopdet]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:Decan star (Ancient Egypt)]] [[Category:WikiProject Ancient Egypt]] [[Category:pages without German translation box]] [[Category:Tepi-a Sopdet]] [[Category:11]] }} [[en:draft:Tepi-a Sopdet]] [[de:Tepi-a Sopdet]] [[sn:Tepi-a-Sopdet]] {{citation needed|Tepi-a-Sopdet}} [[Category:Pages with German translation box]] {{translated page|de|Tepi-a Sopdet|version=210539685|comment=Imported full de history}}

A heliacal observation during the decade was therefore not possible. | style="text-align:center" | {{ill|Senusret III|de|Sesostris III.|qq=draft}} |- bgcolor="#f5f5f5" | style="text-align:center" | 1541 BCE | style="text-align:center" | 19th Peret I

revision.comment: Text replacement - "{{citation needed\|[^]]+}}" to ""
revision.text:
{{Infobox Hieroglyphen |NAME = D1:D36 M44-X1 N14-N33 |NAME-TRANSKRIPTION = Tepi-a Sopdet
''Tpj-ˁ Spd.t''
''Forerunner of Sopdet'' |BILD1 = Position Beta Cma.png |BILD1-BREITE = 250px |BILD1-BESCHREIBUNG = Constellation “Sopdet” ([[Canis Major]]) }} '''Tepi-a Sopdet''' is the designation of an {{ill|Ancient Egypt|de|Altes Ägypten|qq=draft}} {{ill|Decade (Ancient Egypt)|de|Dekade (Altägypten)|1=Decan|qq=draft}}, which comprised only one {{ill|Baktiu|de|Baktiu|qq=draft}} (decan star). == Background == This decan corresponds to {{ill|Murzim|de|Murzim|qq=draft}}, the forerunner of {{ill|Sirius|de|Sirius|qq=draft}}. The traditional {{ill|Arabic language|de|Arabische Sprache|qq=draft}} name, "Harbinger," likely refers to its position, as it rises before Sirius and heralds the brightest star in the night sky. However, this role does not apply to {{ill|Heliacal rising|de|Heliakisch|qq=draft}} because Murzim is significantly dimmer than Sirius and requires a {{ill|Altitude|de|Sehungsbogen|qq=draft}} of at least 13° to become heliacally visible, or approximately 55 minutes before the sun rises. Furthermore, Murzim requires a minimum horizon altitude of 1.7° due to atmospheric refraction. The same conditions apply to {{ill|Acronychal setting|de|Akronychisch|1=Acronychic|qq=draft}}. The entries in the {{ill|Book of Nut|de|Nutbuch|qq=draft}} regarding decan positions can therefore only refer to the acronychal {{ill|Culmination (astronomy)|de|Kulmination (Astronomie)|qq=draft}}, as only in this context could Murzim Rank before Sirius in the decan lists. During the era of the {{ill|Diagonal star clock|de|Diagonalsternuhren|qq=draft}}, Murzim reached a culmination altitude of approximately 36.6° in Ancient Egypt. Sirius itself was part of the Ancient Egyptian constellation {{ill|Sopdet (constellation)|de|Sopdet (Sternbild)|qq=draft}}. {{ill|Alexandra von Lieven|fr|Alexandra von Lieven|de|Alexandra von Lieven|qq=draft}} notes the decan star "Tepi-a Sopdet" mentioned in the {{ill|Book of Nut|de|Nutbuch|qq=draft}}, which in the seventh year of {{ill|Senusret III|de|Sesostris III.|qq=draft}} was listed as the 34th decan with the {{ill|Heliacal rising|de|Heliakisch|qq=draft}} assigned to the {{ill|Peret#Dating|lt=6th Peret IV|de|6. Peret IV|qq=draft}} (approximately June 15/21); the {{ill|Culmination (astronomy)|de|Kulmination (Astronomie)|qq=draft}} for the {{ill|Achet#Dating|lt=26th Achet II|de|26. Achet II|qq=draft}}, and the acronychal setting for the 26th Peret I. The recorded period of invisibility lasted the canonical 70 days.Alexandra von Lieven: ''Grundriss des Laufes der Sterne''. p. 66. == Dating of Tepi-a Sopdet (Book of Nut System) == {| class="wikitable" style="width: 98%; border: 2px solid gray;" |- {{Ägyptologie TblHighlight}} |colspan="7" align=center style="background:#FFE39B"|'''Dating of Tepi-a Sopdet in Memphis''' |- {{Ägyptologie TblHighlight}} !Year ![[Culmination (astronomy)|Acronychal
Culmination]] ![[Acronychal|Acronychal
Setting]] ![[Heliacal rising|Heliacal
Rising]]The altitude values refer to the end of the respective night hour. Heliacal rising occurs before sunrise, acronychal setting, and acronychal culmination after sunset. !Remarks |- bgcolor="#fffaf0" | style="text-align:center" | 1873 BCE | style="text-align:center" | {{ill|Achet#Dating|lt=26th Achet II|de|26. Achet II|qq=draft}}
January 11–12
Altitude 30.3°Actual culmination: 36.5°, approximately 2.75 hours after sunset. | style="text-align:center" | {{ill|Peret#Dating|lt=26th Peret I|de|26. Peret I|qq=draft}}
April 11–12
Altitude 10.4°The observation of the setting was only possible towards the end of the decade. | style="text-align:center" | {{ill|Peret#Dating|lt=6th Peret IV|de|6. Peret IV|qq=draft}}
June 20–21
Altitude 2.3°On the 10th decade day, the altitude was 9.3°. A heliacal observation during the decade was therefore not possible. | style="text-align:center" | {{ill|Senusret III|de|Sesostris III.|qq=draft}} |- bgcolor="#f5f5f5" | style="text-align:center" | 1541 BCE | style="text-align:center" | 19th Peret I
January 14–15 | style="text-align:center" | 19th Peret IV
April 14–15 | style="text-align:center" | {{ill|Season of the Harvest|lt=Shemu#Dating|lt=29th Shemu II|de|29. Schemu II|qq=draft}}
June 23–24 | style="text-align:center" | {{ill|Ebers calendar|de|Ebers-Kalender|qq=draft}} |- bgcolor="#fffaf0" | style="text-align:center" | 1463 BCE | style="text-align:center" | 8th Peret II
January 15–16 | style="text-align:center" | 8th Shemu I
April 15–16 | style="text-align:center" | 18th Shemu III
June 24–25 | style="text-align:center" | {{ill|Ramesside star clocks|de|Ramessidische Sternuhren|qq=draft}} |- |} == Literature == * {{ill|Christian Leitz|fr|Christian Leitz|en|Christian Leitz|de|Christian Leitz}}: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren''. Peeters, Leuven 1995, ISBN 90-6831-669-9, p. 95. * {{ill|Alexandra von Lieven|fr|Alexandra von Lieven|de|Alexandra von Lieven|qq=draft}}: ''Grundriss des Laufes der Sterne – Das sogenannte Nutbuch''. The Carsten Niebuhr Institute of Ancient Eastern Studies (et al.), Copenhagen 2007, ISBN 978-87-635-0406-5, pp. 62 and 398. == References == {{translated page|de|Tepi-a Sopdet|version=210539685|comment=Imported full de history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Pages without meta categories]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Tepi-a-Sopdet]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:Decan star (Ancient Egypt)]] [[Category:WikiProject Ancient Egypt]] [[Category:Tepi-a Sopdet]] [[Category:11]] }} [[sn:Tepi-a-Sopdet]] [[en:draft:Tepi-a Sopdet]] [[de:Tepi-a Sopdet]]

June 24–25 | style="text-align:center" | {{ill|Ramesside star clocks|de|Ramessidische Sternuhren|qq=draft}} |- |} == Literature == * {{ill|Christian Leitz|fr|Christian Leitz|en|Christian Leitz|de|Christian Leitz}}: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren''. Peeters, Leuven 1995, ISBN 90-6831-669-9, p. 95. * {{ill|Alexandra von Lieven|fr|Alexandra von Lieven|de|Alexandra von Lieven|qq=draft}}: ''Grundriss des Laufes der Sterne – Das sogenannte Nutbuch''. revision.text:
{{Infobox hieroglyphs |NAME = D1:D36 M44-X1 N14-N33 |NAME-TRANSKRIPTION = Tepi-a Sopdet
''Tpj-ˁ Spd.t''
''Forerunner of Sopdet'' |BILD1 = Position Beta Cma.png |BILD1-BREITE = 250px |BILD1-BESCHREIBUNG = Constellation “Sopdet” ([[Canis Major]]) }} '''Tepi-a Sopdet''' is the designation of an {{ill|Ancient Egypt|de|Altes Ägypten|qq=draft}} {{ill|Decade (Ancient Egypt)|de|Dekade (Altägypten)|1=Decan|qq=draft}}, which comprised only one {{ill|Baktiu|de|Baktiu|qq=draft}} (decan star). == Background == This decan corresponds to {{ill|Murzim|de|Murzim|qq=draft}}, the forerunner of {{ill|Sirius|de|Sirius|qq=draft}}. The traditional {{ill|Arabic language|de|Arabische Sprache|qq=draft}} name, "Harbinger," likely refers to its position, as it rises before Sirius and heralds the brightest star in the night sky. However, this role does not apply to {{ill|Heliacal rising|de|Heliakisch|qq=draft}} because Murzim is significantly dimmer than Sirius and requires a {{ill|Altitude|de|Sehungsbogen|qq=draft}} of at least 13° to become heliacally visible, or approximately 55 minutes before the sun rises. Furthermore, Murzim requires a minimum horizon altitude of 1.7° due to atmospheric refraction. The same conditions apply to {{ill|Acronychal setting|de|Akronychisch|1=Acronychic|qq=draft}}. The entries in the {{ill|Book of Nut|de|Nutbuch|qq=draft}} regarding decan positions can therefore only refer to the acronychal {{ill|Culmination (astronomy)|de|Kulmination (Astronomie)|qq=draft}}, as only in this context could Murzim Rank before Sirius in the decan lists. During the era of the {{ill|Diagonal star clock|de|Diagonalsternuhren|qq=draft}}, Murzim reached a culmination altitude of approximately 36.6° in Ancient Egypt. Sirius itself was part of the Ancient Egyptian constellation {{ill|Sopdet (constellation)|de|Sopdet (Sternbild)|qq=draft}}. {{ill|Alexandra von Lieven|fr|Alexandra von Lieven|de|Alexandra von Lieven|qq=draft}} notes the decan star "Tepi-a Sopdet" mentioned in the {{ill|Book of Nut|de|Nutbuch|qq=draft}}, which in the seventh year of {{ill|Senusret III|de|Sesostris III.|qq=draft}} was listed as the 34th decan with the {{ill|Heliacal rising|de|Heliakisch|qq=draft}} assigned to the {{ill|Peret#Dating|lt=6th Peret IV|de|6. Peret IV|qq=draft}} (approximately June 15/21); the {{ill|Culmination (astronomy)|de|Kulmination (Astronomie)|qq=draft}} for the {{ill|Achet#Dating|lt=26th Achet II|de|26. Achet II|qq=draft}}, and the acronychal setting for the 26th Peret I. The recorded period of invisibility lasted the canonical 70 days.Alexandra von Lieven: ''Grundriss des Laufes der Sterne''. p. 66. == Dating of Tepi-a Sopdet (Book of Nut System) == {| class="wikitable" style="width: 98%; border: 2px solid gray;" |- {{Ägyptologie TblHighlight}} |colspan="7" align=center style="background:#FFE39B"|'''Dating of Tepi-a Sopdet in Memphis''' |- {{Ägyptologie TblHighlight}} !Year ![[Culmination (astronomy)|Acronychal
Culmination]] ![[Acronychal|Acronychal
Setting]] ![[Heliacal rising|Heliacal
Rising]]The altitude values refer to the end of the respective night hour. Heliacal rising occurs before sunrise, acronychal setting, and acronychal culmination after sunset. !Remarks |- bgcolor="#fffaf0" | style="text-align:center" | 1873 BCE | style="text-align:center" | {{ill|Achet#Dating|lt=26th Achet II|de|26. Achet II|qq=draft}}
January 11–12
Altitude 30.3°Actual culmination: 36.5°, approximately 2.75 hours after sunset. | style="text-align:center" | {{ill|Peret#Dating|lt=26th Peret I|de|26. Peret I|qq=draft}}
April 11–12
Altitude 10.4°The observation of the setting was only possible towards the end of the decade. | style="text-align:center" | {{ill|Peret#Dating|lt=6th Peret IV|de|6. Peret IV|qq=draft}}
June 20–21
Altitude 2.3°On the 10th decade day, the altitude was 9.3°. A heliacal observation during the decade was therefore not possible. | style="text-align:center" | {{ill|Senusret III|de|Sesostris III.|qq=draft}} |- bgcolor="#f5f5f5" | style="text-align:center" | 1541 BCE | style="text-align:center" | 19th Peret I
January 14–15 | style="text-align:center" | 19th Peret IV
April 14–15 | style="text-align:center" | {{ill|Season of the Harvest|lt=Shemu#Dating|lt=29th Shemu II|de|29. Schemu II|qq=draft}}
June 23–24 | style="text-align:center" | {{ill|Ebers calendar|de|Ebers-Kalender|qq=draft}} |- bgcolor="#fffaf0" | style="text-align:center" | 1463 BCE | style="text-align:center" | 8th Peret II
January 15–16 | style="text-align:center" | 8th Shemu I
April 15–16 | style="text-align:center" | 18th Shemu III
June 24–25 | style="text-align:center" | {{ill|Ramesside star clocks|de|Ramessidische Sternuhren|qq=draft}} |- |} == Literature == * {{ill|Christian Leitz|fr|Christian Leitz|en|Christian Leitz|de|Christian Leitz}}: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren''. Peeters, Leuven 1995, ISBN 90-6831-669-9, p. 95. * {{ill|Alexandra von Lieven|fr|Alexandra von Lieven|de|Alexandra von Lieven|qq=draft}}: ''Grundriss des Laufes der Sterne – Das sogenannte Nutbuch''. The Carsten Niebuhr Institute of Ancient Eastern Studies (et al.), Copenhagen 2007, ISBN 978-87-635-0406-5, pp. 62 and 398. == References == [[Category:translated pages without meta categories]] {{translated page|de|Tepi-a Sopdet|version=210539685|comment=Imported full de history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:qq]][[sn:Tepi-a-Sopdet]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:Decan star (Ancient Egypt)]] [[Category:WikiProject Ancient Egypt]] [[Category:Tepi-a Sopdet]] [[Category:11]] }} [[sn:Tepi-a-Sopdet]] [[en:draft:Tepi-a Sopdet]] [[de:Tepi-a Sopdet]]

The Carsten Niebuhr Institute of Ancient Eastern Studies (et al.), Copenhagen 2007, ISBN 978-87-635-0406-5, pp. 62 and 398. == References == [[Category:completion tier 1]] {{translated page|de|Tepi-a Sopdet|version=210539685|comment=Imported full de history}} {{draft categories| [[sn:Tepi-a-Sopdet]][[Category:Decan star (Ancient Egypt)]] [[Category:WikiProject Ancient Egypt]] [[Category:Tepi-a Sopdet]] }} [[sn:Tepi-a-Sopdet]] [[en:draft:Tepi-a Sopdet]] [[de:Tepi-a Sopdet]]

revision.comment: Bot: add simplewiki draft link to ill templates
revision.text:
{{Infobox hieroglyphs |NAME = D1:D36 M44-X1 N14-N33 |NAME-TRANSKRIPTION = Tepi-a Sopdet
''Tpj-ˁ Spd.t''
''Forerunner of Sopdet'' |BILD1 = Position Beta Cma.png |BILD1-BREITE = 250px |BILD1-BESCHREIBUNG = Constellation “Sopdet” ([[Canis Major]]) }} '''Tepi-a Sopdet''' is the designation of an {{ill|Ancient Egypt|de|Altes Ägypten|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ancient Egypt|qq=}} {{ill|Decade (Ancient Egypt)|de|Dekade (Altägypten)|1=Decan|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Decade (Ancient Egypt)|13=User:Immanuelle/Decan|qq=}}, which comprised only one {{ill|Baktiu|de|Baktiu|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Baktiu|qq=}} (decan star). == Background == This decan corresponds to {{ill|Murzim|de|Murzim|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Murzim|qq=}}, the forerunner of {{ill|Sirius|de|Sirius|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sirius|qq=}}. The traditional {{ill|Arabic language|de|Arabische Sprache|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Arabic language|qq=}} name, "Harbinger," likely refers to its position, as it rises before Sirius and heralds the brightest star in the night sky. However, this role does not apply to {{ill|Heliacal rising|de|Heliakisch|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Heliacal rising|qq=}} because Murzim is significantly dimmer than Sirius and requires a {{ill|Altitude|de|Sehungsbogen|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Altitude|qq=}} of at least 13° to become heliacally visible, or approximately 55 minutes before the sun rises. Furthermore, Murzim requires a minimum horizon altitude of 1.7° due to atmospheric refraction. The same conditions apply to {{ill|Acronychal setting|de|Akronychisch|1=Acronychic|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Acronychal setting|13=User:Immanuelle/Acronychic|qq=}}. The entries in the {{ill|Book of Nut|de|Nutbuch|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Book of Nut|qq=}} regarding decan positions can therefore only refer to the acronychal {{ill|Culmination (astronomy)|de|Kulmination (Astronomie)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Culmination (astronomy)|qq=}}, as only in this context could Murzim Rank before Sirius in the decan lists. During the era of the {{ill|Diagonal star clock|de|Diagonalsternuhren|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Diagonal star clock|qq=}}, Murzim reached a culmination altitude of approximately 36.6° in Ancient Egypt. Sirius itself was part of the Ancient Egyptian constellation {{ill|Sopdet (constellation)|de|Sopdet (Sternbild)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sopdet (constellation)|qq=}}. {{ill|Alexandra von Lieven|fr|Alexandra von Lieven|de|Alexandra von Lieven|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Alexandra von Lieven|qq=}} notes the decan star "Tepi-a Sopdet" mentioned in the {{ill|Book of Nut|de|Nutbuch|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Book of Nut|qq=}}, which in the seventh year of {{ill|Senusret III|de|Sesostris III.|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Senusret III|qq=}} was listed as the 34th decan with the {{ill|Heliacal rising|de|Heliakisch|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Heliacal rising|qq=}} assigned to the {{ill|Peret#Dating|lt=6th Peret IV|de|6. Peret IV|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Peret#Dating|qq=}} (approximately June 15/21); the {{ill|Culmination (astronomy)|de|Kulmination (Astronomie)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Culmination (astronomy)|qq=}} for the {{ill|Achet#Dating|lt=26th Achet II|de|26. Achet II|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Achet#Dating|qq=}}, and the acronychal setting for the 26th Peret I. The recorded period of invisibility lasted the canonical 70 days.Alexandra von Lieven: ''Grundriss des Laufes der Sterne''. p. 66. == Dating of Tepi-a Sopdet (Book of Nut System) == {| class="wikitable" style="width: 98%; border: 2px solid gray;" |- {{Ägyptologie TblHighlight}} |colspan="7" align=center style="background:#FFE39B"|'''Dating of Tepi-a Sopdet in Memphis''' |- {{Ägyptologie TblHighlight}} !Year ![[Culmination (astronomy)|Acronychal
Culmination]] ![[Acronychal|Acronychal
Setting]] ![[Heliacal rising|Heliacal
Rising]]The altitude values refer to the end of the respective night hour. Heliacal rising occurs before sunrise, acronychal setting, and acronychal culmination after sunset. !Remarks |- bgcolor="#fffaf0" | style="text-align:center" | 1873 BCE | style="text-align:center" | {{ill|Achet#Dating|lt=26th Achet II|de|26. Achet II|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Achet#Dating|qq=}}
January 11–12
Altitude 30.3°Actual culmination: 36.5°, approximately 2.75 hours after sunset. | style="text-align:center" | {{ill|Peret#Dating|lt=26th Peret I|de|26. Peret I|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Peret#Dating|qq=}}
April 11–12
Altitude 10.4°The observation of the setting was only possible towards the end of the decade. | style="text-align:center" | {{ill|Peret#Dating|lt=6th Peret IV|de|6. Peret IV|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Peret#Dating|qq=}}
June 20–21
Altitude 2.3°On the 10th decade day, the altitude was 9.3°. A heliacal observation during the decade was therefore not possible. | style="text-align:center" | {{ill|Senusret III|de|Sesostris III.|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Senusret III|qq=}} |- bgcolor="#f5f5f5" | style="text-align:center" | 1541 BCE | style="text-align:center" | 19th Peret I
January 14–15 | style="text-align:center" | 19th Peret IV
April 14–15 | style="text-align:center" | {{ill|Season of the Harvest|lt=Shemu#Dating|lt=29th Shemu II|de|29. Schemu II|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Season of the Harvest|qq=}}
June 23–24 | style="text-align:center" | {{ill|Ebers calendar|de|Ebers-Kalender|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ebers calendar|qq=}} |- bgcolor="#fffaf0" | style="text-align:center" | 1463 BCE | style="text-align:center" | 8th Peret II
January 15–16 | style="text-align:center" | 8th Shemu I
April 15–16 | style="text-align:center" | 18th Shemu III
June 24–25 | style="text-align:center" | {{ill|Ramesside star clocks|de|Ramessidische Sternuhren|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ramesside star clocks|qq=}} |- |} == Literature == * {{ill|Christian Leitz|fr|Christian Leitz|en|Christian Leitz|de|Christian Leitz|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Christian Leitz|qq=}}: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren''. Peeters, Leuven 1995, ISBN 90-6831-669-9, p. 95. * {{ill|Alexandra von Lieven|fr|Alexandra von Lieven|de|Alexandra von Lieven|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Alexandra von Lieven|qq=}}: ''Grundriss des Laufes der Sterne – Das sogenannte Nutbuch''. The Carsten Niebuhr Institute of Ancient Eastern Studies (et al.), Copenhagen 2007, ISBN 978-87-635-0406-5, pp. 62 and 398. == References == [[Category:completion tier 1]] {{translated page|de|Tepi-a Sopdet|version=210539685|comment=Imported full de history}} {{draft categories| [[sn:Tepi-a-Sopdet]][[Category:Decan star (Ancient Egypt)]] [[Category:WikiProject Ancient Egypt]] [[Category:Tepi-a Sopdet]] }} [[sn:Tepi-a-Sopdet]] [[en:draft:Tepi-a Sopdet]] [[de:Tepi-a Sopdet]]

revision.text:
{{Infobox hieroglyphs |NAME = D1:D36 M44-X1 N14-N33 |NAME-TRANSKRIPTION = Tepi-a Sopdet
''Tpj-ˁ Spd.t''
''Forerunner of Sopdet'' |BILD1 = Position Beta Cma.png |BILD1-BREITE = 250px |BILD1-BESCHREIBUNG = Constellation “Sopdet” ([[Canis Major]]) }} '''Tepi-a Sopdet''' is the designation of an {{ill|Ancient Egypt|de|Altes Ägypten|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ancient Egypt|qq=}} {{ill|Decade (Ancient Egypt)|de|Dekade (Altägypten)|1=Decan|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Decade (Ancient Egypt)|13=User:Immanuelle/Decan|qq=}}, which comprised only one {{ill|Baktiu|de|Baktiu|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Baktiu|qq=}} (decan star). == Background == This decan corresponds to {{ill|Murzim|de|Murzim|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Murzim|qq=}}, the forerunner of {{ill|Sirius|de|Sirius|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sirius|qq=}}. The traditional {{ill|Arabic language|de|Arabische Sprache|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Arabic language|qq=}} name, "Harbinger," likely refers to its position, as it rises before Sirius and heralds the brightest star in the night sky. However, this role does not apply to {{ill|Heliacal rising|de|Heliakisch|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Heliacal rising|qq=}} because Murzim is significantly dimmer than Sirius and requires a {{ill|Altitude|de|Sehungsbogen|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Altitude|qq=}} of at least 13° to become heliacally visible, or approximately 55 minutes before the sun rises. Furthermore, Murzim requires a minimum horizon altitude of 1.7° due to atmospheric refraction. The same conditions apply to {{ill|Acronychal setting|de|Akronychisch|1=Acronychic|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Acronychal setting|13=User:Immanuelle/Acronychic|qq=}}. The entries in the {{ill|Book of Nut|de|Nutbuch|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Book of Nut|qq=}} regarding decan positions can therefore only refer to the acronychal {{ill|Culmination (astronomy)|de|Kulmination (Astronomie)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Culmination (astronomy)|qq=}}, as only in this context could Murzim Rank before Sirius in the decan lists. During the era of the {{ill|Diagonal star clock|de|Diagonalsternuhren|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Diagonal star clock|qq=}}, Murzim reached a culmination altitude of approximately 36.6° in Ancient Egypt. Sirius itself was part of the Ancient Egyptian constellation {{ill|Sopdet (constellation)|de|Sopdet (Sternbild)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sopdet (constellation)|qq=}}. {{ill|Alexandra von Lieven|fr|Alexandra von Lieven|de|Alexandra von Lieven|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Alexandra von Lieven|qq=}} notes the decan star "Tepi-a Sopdet" mentioned in the {{ill|Book of Nut|de|Nutbuch|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Book of Nut|qq=}}, which in the seventh year of {{ill|Senusret III|de|Sesostris III.|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Senusret III|qq=}} was listed as the 34th decan with the {{ill|Heliacal rising|de|Heliakisch|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Heliacal rising|qq=}} assigned to the {{ill|Peret#Dating|lt=6th Peret IV|de|6. Peret IV|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Peret#Dating|qq=}} (approximately June 15/21); the {{ill|Culmination (astronomy)|de|Kulmination (Astronomie)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Culmination (astronomy)|qq=}} for the {{ill|Achet#Dating|lt=26th Achet II|de|26. Achet II|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Achet#Dating|qq=}}, and the acronychal setting for the 26th Peret I. The recorded period of invisibility lasted the canonical 70 days.Alexandra von Lieven: ''Grundriss des Laufes der Sterne''. p. 66. == Dating of Tepi-a Sopdet (Book of Nut System) == {| class="wikitable" style="width: 98%; border: 2px solid gray;" |- {{Ägyptologie TblHighlight}} |colspan="7" align=center style="background:#FFE39B"|'''Dating of Tepi-a Sopdet in Memphis''' |- {{Ägyptologie TblHighlight}} !Year ![[Culmination (astronomy)|Acronychal
Culmination]] ![[Acronychal|Acronychal
Setting]] ![[Heliacal rising|Heliacal
Rising]]The altitude values refer to the end of the respective night hour. Heliacal rising occurs before sunrise, acronychal setting, and acronychal culmination after sunset. !Remarks |- bgcolor="#fffaf0" | style="text-align:center" | 1873 BCE | style="text-align:center" | {{ill|Achet#Dating|lt=26th Achet II|de|26. Achet II|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Achet#Dating|qq=}}
January 11–12
Altitude 30.3°Actual culmination: 36.5°, approximately 2.75 hours after sunset. | style="text-align:center" | {{ill|Peret#Dating|lt=26th Peret I|de|26. Peret I|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Peret#Dating|qq=}}
April 11–12
Altitude 10.4°The observation of the setting was only possible towards the end of the decade. | style="text-align:center" | {{ill|Peret#Dating|lt=6th Peret IV|de|6. Peret IV|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Peret#Dating|qq=}}
June 20–21
Altitude 2.3°On the 10th decade day, the altitude was 9.3°. A heliacal observation during the decade was therefore not possible. | style="text-align:center" | {{ill|Senusret III|de|Sesostris III.|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Senusret III|qq=}} |- bgcolor="#f5f5f5" | style="text-align:center" | 1541 BCE | style="text-align:center" | 19th Peret I
January 14–15 | style="text-align:center" | 19th Peret IV
April 14–15 | style="text-align:center" | {{ill|Season of the Harvest|lt=Shemu#Dating|lt=29th Shemu II|de|29. Schemu II|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Season of the Harvest|qq=}}
June 23–24 | style="text-align:center" | {{ill|Ebers calendar|de|Ebers-Kalender|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ebers calendar|qq=}} |- bgcolor="#fffaf0" | style="text-align:center" | 1463 BCE | style="text-align:center" | 8th Peret II
January 15–16 | style="text-align:center" | 8th Shemu I
April 15–16 | style="text-align:center" | 18th Shemu III
June 24–25 | style="text-align:center" | {{ill|Ramesside star clocks|de|Ramessidische Sternuhren|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ramesside star clocks|qq=}} |- |} == Literature == * {{ill|Christian Leitz|fr|Christian Leitz|en|Christian Leitz|de|Christian Leitz|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Christian Leitz|qq=}}: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren''. Peeters, Leuven 1995, ISBN 90-6831-669-9, p. 95. * {{ill|Alexandra von Lieven|fr|Alexandra von Lieven|de|Alexandra von Lieven|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Alexandra von Lieven|qq=}}: ''Grundriss des Laufes der Sterne – Das sogenannte Nutbuch''. The Carsten Niebuhr Institute of Ancient Eastern Studies (et al.), Copenhagen 2007, ISBN 978-87-635-0406-5, pp. 62 and 398. == References == [[Category:completion tier 1]] {{translated page|de|Tepi-a Sopdet|version=210539685|comment=Imported full de history}} {{draft categories| [[sn:Tepi-a-Sopdet]][[Category:Ancient Egyptian Decan Stars]] [[Category:WikiProject Ancient Egypt]][[sn:Tepi-a-Sopdet]][[Category:WikiProject Ancient Egypt]] [[Category:WikiProject Ancient Egypt]] [[Category:Tepi-a Sopdet]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Tepi-a-Sopdet]][[Category:qq]] }} [[sn:Tepi-a-Sopdet]] [[en:draft:Tepi-a Sopdet]] [[de:Tepi-a Sopdet]]

revision.text:
{{Infobox hieroglyphs |NAME = D1:D36 M44-X1 N14-N33 |NAME-TRANSKRIPTION = Tepi-a Sopdet
''Tpj-ˁ Spd.t''
''Forerunner of Sopdet'' |BILD1 = Position Beta Cma.png |BILD1-BREITE = 250px |BILD1-BESCHREIBUNG = Constellation “Sopdet” ([[Canis Major]]) }} '''Tepi-a Sopdet''' is the designation of an [[:en:Ancient Egypt|Ancient Egypt]] [[:en:Decan|Decan]], which comprised only one {{ill|Baktiu|de|Baktiu|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Baktiu|qq=}} (decan star). == Background == This decan corresponds to [[:en:Beta Canis Majoris|Murzim]], the forerunner of [[:en:Sirius|Sirius]]. The traditional [[:en:Arabic|Arabic language]] name, "Harbinger," likely refers to its position, as it rises before Sirius and heralds the brightest star in the night sky. However, this role does not apply to [[:en:Heliacal rising|Heliacal rising]] because Murzim is significantly dimmer than Sirius and requires a {{ill|Altitude|de|Sehungsbogen|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Altitude|qq=}} of at least 13° to become heliacally visible, or approximately 55 minutes before the sun rises. Furthermore, Murzim requires a minimum horizon altitude of 1.7° due to atmospheric refraction. The same conditions apply to {{ill|Acronychal setting|de|Akronychisch|1=Acronychic|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Acronychal setting|13=User:Immanuelle/Acronychic|qq=}}. The entries in the [[:en:Book of Nut|Book of Nut]] regarding decan positions can therefore only refer to the acronychal [[:en:Culmination|Culmination (astronomy)]], as only in this context could Murzim Rank before Sirius in the decan lists. During the era of the {{ill|Diagonal star clock|de|Diagonalsternuhren|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Diagonal star clock|qq=}}, Murzim reached a culmination altitude of approximately 36.6° in Ancient Egypt. Sirius itself was part of the Ancient Egyptian constellation {{ill|Sopdet (constellation)|de|Sopdet (Sternbild)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sopdet (constellation)|qq=}}. {{ill|Alexandra von Lieven|fr|Alexandra von Lieven|de|Alexandra von Lieven|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Alexandra von Lieven|qq=}} notes the decan star "Tepi-a Sopdet" mentioned in the [[:en:Book of Nut|Book of Nut]], which in the seventh year of [[:en:Senusret III|Senusret III]] was listed as the 34th decan with the [[:en:Heliacal rising|Heliacal rising]] assigned to the {{ill|Peret#Dating|lt=6th Peret IV|de|6. Peret IV|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Peret#Dating|qq=}} (approximately June 15/21); the [[:en:Culmination|Culmination (astronomy)]] for the {{ill|Achet#Dating|lt=26th Achet II|de|26. Achet II|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Achet#Dating|qq=}}, and the acronychal setting for the 26th Peret I. The recorded period of invisibility lasted the canonical 70 days.Alexandra von Lieven: ''Grundriss des Laufes der Sterne''. p. 66. == Dating of Tepi-a Sopdet (Book of Nut System) == {| class="wikitable" style="width: 98%; border: 2px solid gray;" |- {{Ägyptologie TblHighlight}} |colspan="7" align=center style="background:#FFE39B"|'''Dating of Tepi-a Sopdet in Memphis''' |- {{Ägyptologie TblHighlight}} !Year ![[Culmination (astronomy)|Acronychal
Culmination]] ![[Acronychal|Acronychal
Setting]] ![[Heliacal rising|Heliacal
Rising]]The altitude values refer to the end of the respective night hour. Heliacal rising occurs before sunrise, acronychal setting, and acronychal culmination after sunset. !Remarks |- bgcolor="#fffaf0" | style="text-align:center" | 1873 BCE | style="text-align:center" | {{ill|Achet#Dating|lt=26th Achet II|de|26. Achet II|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Achet#Dating|qq=}}
January 11–12
Altitude 30.3°Actual culmination: 36.5°, approximately 2.75 hours after sunset. | style="text-align:center" | {{ill|Peret#Dating|lt=26th Peret I|de|26. Peret I|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Peret#Dating|qq=}}
April 11–12
Altitude 10.4°The observation of the setting was only possible towards the end of the decade. | style="text-align:center" | {{ill|Peret#Dating|lt=6th Peret IV|de|6. Peret IV|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Peret#Dating|qq=}}
June 20–21
Altitude 2.3°On the 10th decade day, the altitude was 9.3°. A heliacal observation during the decade was therefore not possible. | style="text-align:center" | [[:en:Senusret III|Senusret III]] |- bgcolor="#f5f5f5" | style="text-align:center" | 1541 BCE | style="text-align:center" | 19th Peret I
January 14–15 | style="text-align:center" | 19th Peret IV
April 14–15 | style="text-align:center" | {{ill|Season of the Harvest|lt=Shemu#Dating|lt=29th Shemu II|de|29. Schemu II|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Season of the Harvest|qq=}}
June 23–24 | style="text-align:center" | {{ill|Ebers calendar|de|Ebers-Kalender|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ebers calendar|qq=}} |- bgcolor="#fffaf0" | style="text-align:center" | 1463 BCE | style="text-align:center" | 8th Peret II
January 15–16 | style="text-align:center" | 8th Shemu I
April 15–16 | style="text-align:center" | 18th Shemu III
June 24–25 | style="text-align:center" | {{ill|Ramesside star clocks|de|Ramessidische Sternuhren|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ramesside star clocks|qq=}} |- |} == Literature == * [[:en:Christian Leitz|Christian Leitz]]: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren''. Peeters, Leuven 1995, ISBN 90-6831-669-9, p. 95. * {{ill|Alexandra von Lieven|fr|Alexandra von Lieven|de|Alexandra von Lieven|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Alexandra von Lieven|qq=}}: ''Grundriss des Laufes der Sterne – Das sogenannte Nutbuch''. The Carsten Niebuhr Institute of Ancient Eastern Studies (et al.), Copenhagen 2007, ISBN 978-87-635-0406-5, pp. 62 and 398. == References == [[Category:completion tier 1]] {{translated page|de|Tepi-a Sopdet|version=210539685|comment=Imported full de history}} {{draft categories| [[sn:Tepi-a-Sopdet]][[Category:Ancient Egyptian Decan Stars]] [[Category:WikiProject Ancient Egypt]][[sn:Tepi-a-Sopdet]][[Category:WikiProject Ancient Egypt]] [[Category:WikiProject Ancient Egypt]] [[Category:Tepi-a Sopdet]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Tepi-a-Sopdet]][[Category:qq]] }} [[sn:Tepi-a-Sopdet]] [[en:draft:Tepi-a Sopdet]] [[de:Tepi-a Sopdet]]

revision.text:
{{Infobox hieroglyphs |NAME = D1:D36 M44-X1 N14-N33 |NAME-TRANSKRIPTION = Tepi-a Sopdet
''Tpj-ˁ Spd.t''
''Forerunner of Sopdet'' |BILD1 = Position Beta Cma.png |BILD1-BREITE = 250px |BILD1-BESCHREIBUNG = Constellation “Sopdet” ([[Canis Major]]) }} '''Tepi-a Sopdet''' is the designation of an [[:en:Ancient Egypt|Ancient Egypt]] [[:en:Decan|Decan]], which comprised only one {{ill|Baktiu|de|Baktiu|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Baktiu|qq=}} (decan star). == Background == This decan corresponds to [[:en:Beta Canis Majoris|Murzim]], the forerunner of [[:en:Sirius|Sirius]]. The traditional [[:en:Arabic|Arabic language]] name, "Harbinger," likely refers to its position, as it rises before Sirius and heralds the brightest star in the night sky. However, this role does not apply to [[:en:Heliacal rising|Heliacal rising]] because Murzim is significantly dimmer than Sirius and requires a {{ill|Altitude|de|Sehungsbogen|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Altitude|qq=}} of at least 13° to become heliacally visible, or approximately 55 minutes before the sun rises. Furthermore, Murzim requires a minimum horizon altitude of 1.7° due to atmospheric refraction. The same conditions apply to {{ill|Acronychal setting|de|Akronychisch|1=Acronychic|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Acronychal setting|13=User:Immanuelle/Acronychic|qq=}}. The entries in the [[:en:Book of Nut|Book of Nut]] regarding decan positions can therefore only refer to the acronychal [[:en:Culmination|Culmination (astronomy)]], as only in this context could Murzim Rank before Sirius in the decan lists. During the era of the {{ill|Diagonal star clock|de|Diagonalsternuhren|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Diagonal star clock|qq=}}, Murzim reached a culmination altitude of approximately 36.6° in Ancient Egypt. Sirius itself was part of the Ancient Egyptian constellation {{ill|Sopdet (constellation)|de|Sopdet (Sternbild)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sopdet (constellation)|qq=}}. {{ill|Alexandra von Lieven|fr|Alexandra von Lieven|de|Alexandra von Lieven|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Alexandra von Lieven|qq=}} notes the decan star "Tepi-a Sopdet" mentioned in the [[:en:Book of Nut|Book of Nut]], which in the seventh year of [[:en:Senusret III|Senusret III]] was listed as the 34th decan with the [[:en:Heliacal rising|Heliacal rising]] assigned to the {{ill|Peret#Dating|lt=6th Peret IV|de|6. Peret IV|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Peret#Dating|qq=}} (approximately June 15/21); the [[:en:Culmination|Culmination (astronomy)]] for the {{ill|Achet#Dating|lt=26th Achet II|de|26. Achet II|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Achet#Dating|qq=}}, and the acronychal setting for the 26th Peret I. The recorded period of invisibility lasted the canonical 70 days.Alexandra von Lieven: ''Grundriss des Laufes der Sterne''. p. 66. == Dating of Tepi-a Sopdet (Book of Nut System) == {| class="wikitable" style="width: 98%; border: 2px solid gray;" |- {{Ägyptologie TblHighlight}} |colspan="7" align=center style="background:#FFE39B"|'''Dating of Tepi-a Sopdet in Memphis''' |- {{Ägyptologie TblHighlight}} !Year ![[Culmination (astronomy)|Acronychal
Culmination]] ![[Acronychal|Acronychal
Setting]] ![[Heliacal rising|Heliacal
Rising]]The altitude values refer to the end of the respective night hour. Heliacal rising occurs before sunrise, acronychal setting, and acronychal culmination after sunset. !Remarks |- bgcolor="#fffaf0" | style="text-align:center" | 1873 BCE | style="text-align:center" | {{ill|Achet#Dating|lt=26th Achet II|de|26. Achet II|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Achet#Dating|qq=}}
January 11–12
Altitude 30.3°Actual culmination: 36.5°, approximately 2.75 hours after sunset. | style="text-align:center" | {{ill|Peret#Dating|lt=26th Peret I|de|26. Peret I|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Peret#Dating|qq=}}
April 11–12
Altitude 10.4°The observation of the setting was only possible towards the end of the decade. | style="text-align:center" | {{ill|Peret#Dating|lt=6th Peret IV|de|6. Peret IV|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Peret#Dating|qq=}}
June 20–21
Altitude 2.3°On the 10th decade day, the altitude was 9.3°. A heliacal observation during the decade was therefore not possible. | style="text-align:center" | [[:en:Senusret III|Senusret III]] |- bgcolor="#f5f5f5" | style="text-align:center" | 1541 BCE | style="text-align:center" | 19th Peret I
January 14–15 | style="text-align:center" | 19th Peret IV
April 14–15 | style="text-align:center" | {{ill|Season of the Harvest|lt=Shemu#Dating|lt=29th Shemu II|de|29. Schemu II|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Season of the Harvest|qq=}}
June 23–24 | style="text-align:center" | {{ill|Ebers calendar|de|Ebers-Kalender|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ebers calendar|qq=}} |- bgcolor="#fffaf0" | style="text-align:center" | 1463 BCE | style="text-align:center" | 8th Peret II
January 15–16 | style="text-align:center" | 8th Shemu I
April 15–16 | style="text-align:center" | 18th Shemu III
June 24–25 | style="text-align:center" | {{ill|Star clock|lt=Star clock|lt=Ramesside star clocks|de|Ramessidische Sternuhren|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ramesside star clocks|qq=}} |- |} == Literature == * [[:en:Christian Leitz|Christian Leitz]]: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren''. Peeters, Leuven 1995, ISBN 90-6831-669-9, p. 95. * {{ill|Alexandra von Lieven|fr|Alexandra von Lieven|de|Alexandra von Lieven|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Alexandra von Lieven|qq=}}: ''Grundriss des Laufes der Sterne – Das sogenannte Nutbuch''. The Carsten Niebuhr Institute of Ancient Eastern Studies (et al.), Copenhagen 2007, ISBN 978-87-635-0406-5, pp. 62 and 398. == References == [[Category:completion tier 1]] {{translated page|de|Tepi-a Sopdet|version=210539685|comment=Imported full de history}} {{draft categories| [[sn:Tepi-a-Sopdet]][[Category:Ancient Egyptian Decan Stars]] [[Category:WikiProject Ancient Egypt]][[sn:Tepi-a-Sopdet]][[Category:WikiProject Ancient Egypt]] [[Category:WikiProject Ancient Egypt]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Tepi-a-Sopdet]][[Category:qq]] }} [[sn:Tepi-a-Sopdet]] [[en:draft:Tepi-a Sopdet]] [[de:Tepi-a Sopdet]] [[Category:Dekanstern (Altägypten)]]

The same conditions apply to {{ill|Acronychal setting|de|Akronychisch|1=Acronychic|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Acronychal setting|13=User:Immanuelle/Acronychic|qq=}}. The entries in the [[:en:Book of Nut|Book of Nut]] regarding decan positions can therefore only refer to the acronychal [[:en:Culmination|Culmination (astronomy)]], as only in this context could Murzim Rank before Sirius in the decan lists. During the era of the {{ill|Diagonal star clock|de|Diagonalsternuhren|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Diagonal star clock|qq=}}, Murzim reached a culmination altitude of approximately 36.6° in Ancient Egypt. Sirius itself was part of the Ancient Egyptian constellation {{ill|Sopdet (constellation)|de|Sopdet (Sternbild)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sopdet (constellation)|qq=}}. {{ill|Alexandra von Lieven|fr|Alexandra von Lieven|de|Alexandra von Lieven|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Alexandra von Lieven|qq=}} notes the decan star "Tepi-a Sopdet" mentioned in the [[:en:Book of Nut|Book of Nut]], which in the seventh year of [[:en:Senusret III|Senusret III]] was listed as the 34th decan with the [[:en:Heliacal rising|Heliacal rising]] assigned to the {{ill|Peret#Dating|lt=6th Peret IV|de|6. Peret IV|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Peret#Dating|qq=}} (approximately June 15/21); the [[:en:Culmination|Culmination (astronomy)]] for the {{ill|Achet#Dating|lt=26th Achet II|de|26. Achet II|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Achet#Dating|qq=}}, and the acronychal setting for the 26th Peret I. The recorded period of invisibility lasted the canonical 70 days.Alexandra von Lieven: ''Grundriss des Laufes der Sterne''. p. 66. == Dating of Tepi-a Sopdet (Book of Nut System) == {| class="wikitable" style="width: 98%; border: 2px solid gray;" |- {{Ägyptologie TblHighlight}} |colspan="7" align=center style="background:#FFE39B"|'''Dating of Tepi-a Sopdet in Memphis''' |- {{Ägyptologie TblHighlight}} !Year ![[Culmination (astronomy)|Acronychal
Culmination]] ![[Acronychal|Acronychal
Setting]] ![[Heliacal rising|Heliacal
Rising]]The altitude values refer to the end of the respective night hour. Heliacal rising occurs before sunrise, acronychal setting, and acronychal culmination after sunset. !Remarks |- bgcolor="#fffaf0" | style="text-align:center" | 1873 BCE | style="text-align:center" | {{ill|Achet#Dating|lt=26th Achet II|de|26. Achet II|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Achet#Dating|qq=}}
January 11–12
Altitude 30.3°Actual culmination: 36.5°, approximately 2.75 hours after sunset. | style="text-align:center" | {{ill|Peret#Dating|lt=26th Peret I|de|26. Peret I|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Peret#Dating|qq=}}
April 11–12
Altitude 10.4°The observation of the setting was only possible towards the end of the decade. | style="text-align:center" | {{ill|Peret#Dating|lt=6th Peret IV|de|6. Peret IV|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Peret#Dating|qq=}}
June 20–21
Altitude 2.3°On the 10th decade day, the altitude was 9.3°. A heliacal observation during the decade was therefore not possible. | style="text-align:center" | [[:en:Senusret III|Senusret III]] |- bgcolor="#f5f5f5" | style="text-align:center" | 1541 BCE | style="text-align:center" | 19th Peret I
January 14–15 | style="text-align:center" | 19th Peret IV
April 14–15 | style="text-align:center" | {{ill|Season of the Harvest|lt=Shemu#Dating|lt=29th Shemu II|de|29. Schemu II|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Season of the Harvest|qq=}}
June 23–24 | style="text-align:center" | {{ill|Ebers calendar|de|Ebers-Kalender|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ebers calendar|qq=}} |- bgcolor="#fffaf0" | style="text-align:center" | 1463 BCE | style="text-align:center" | 8th Peret II
January 15–16 | style="text-align:center" | 8th Shemu I
April 15–16 | style="text-align:center" | 18th Shemu III
June 24–25 | style="text-align:center" | {{ill|Star clock|lt=Star clock|lt=Ramesside star clocks|de|Ramessidische Sternuhren|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ramesside star clocks|qq=}} |- |} == Literature == * [[:en:Christian Leitz|Christian Leitz]]: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren''. Peeters, Leuven 1995, ISBN 90-6831-669-9, p. 95. * {{ill|Alexandra von Lieven|fr|Alexandra von Lieven|de|Alexandra von Lieven|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Alexandra von Lieven|qq=}}: ''Grundriss des Laufes der Sterne – Das sogenannte Nutbuch''. The Carsten Niebuhr Institute of Ancient Eastern Studies (et al.), Copenhagen 2007, ISBN 978-87-635-0406-5, pp. 62 and 398. == References == [[Category:completion tier 1]] {{translated page|de|Tepi-a Sopdet|version=210539685|comment=Imported full de history}} {{draft categories| [[sn:Tepi-a-Sopdet]][[Category:Ancient Egyptian Decan Stars]] [[Category:WikiProject Ancient Egypt]][[sn:Tepi-a-Sopdet]][[Category:WikiProject Ancient Egypt]] [[Category:WikiProject Ancient Egypt]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Tepi-a-Sopdet]][[Category:qq]] }} [[sn:Tepi-a-Sopdet]] [[de:Tepi-a Sopdet]] [[Category:Ancient Egyptian Decan Stars]]

{{Infobox hieroglyphs |NAME = D1:D36 M44-X1 N14-N33 |NAME-TRANSKRIPTION = Tepi-a Sopdet
''Tpj-ˁ Spd.t''
''Forerunner of Sopdet'' |BILD1 = Position Beta Cma.png |BILD1-BREITE = 250px |BILD1-BESCHREIBUNG = Constellation “Sopdet” ([[:en:Canis Major|Canis Major]]) }} '''Tepi-a Sopdet''' is the designation of an [[:en:Ancient Egypt|Ancient Egypt]] [[:en:Decan|Decan]], which comprised only one {{ill|Baktiu|de|Baktiu|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Baktiu|qq=}} (decan star). == Background == This decan corresponds to [[:en:Beta Canis Majoris|Murzim]], the forerunner of [[:en:Sirius|Sirius]]. The traditional [[:en:Arabic|Arabic language]] name, "Harbinger," likely refers to its position, as it rises before Sirius and heralds the brightest star in the night sky. However, this role does not apply to [[:en:Heliacal rising|Heliacal rising]] because Murzim is significantly dimmer than Sirius and requires a {{ill|Altitude|de|Sehungsbogen|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Altitude|qq=}} of at least 13° to become heliacally visible, or approximately 55 minutes before the sun rises. Furthermore, Murzim requires a minimum horizon altitude of 1.7° due to atmospheric refraction. The same conditions apply to {{ill|Acronychal setting|de|Akronychisch|1=Acronychic|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Acronychal setting|13=User:Immanuelle/Acronychic|qq=}}. The entries in the [[:en:Book of Nut|Book of Nut]] regarding decan positions can therefore only refer to the acronychal [[:en:Culmination|Culmination (astronomy)]], as only in this context could Murzim Rank before Sirius in the decan lists. During the era of the {{ill|Diagonal star clock|de|Diagonalsternuhren|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Diagonal star clock|qq=}}, Murzim reached a culmination altitude of approximately 36.6° in Ancient Egypt. Sirius itself was part of the Ancient Egyptian constellation {{ill|Sopdet (constellation)|de|Sopdet (Sternbild)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sopdet (constellation)|qq=}}. {{ill|Alexandra von Lieven|fr|Alexandra von Lieven|de|Alexandra von Lieven|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Alexandra von Lieven|qq=}} notes the decan star "Tepi-a Sopdet" mentioned in the [[:en:Book of Nut|Book of Nut]], which in the seventh year of [[:en:Senusret III|Senusret III]] was listed as the 34th decan with the [[:en:Heliacal rising|Heliacal rising]] assigned to the {{ill|Peret#Dating|lt=6th Peret IV|de|6. Peret IV|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Peret#Dating|qq=}} (approximately June 15/21); the [[:en:Culmination|Culmination (astronomy)]] for the {{ill|Achet#Dating|lt=26th Achet II|de|26. Achet II|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Achet#Dating|qq=}}, and the acronychal setting for the 26th Peret I. The recorded period of invisibility lasted the canonical 70 days.Alexandra von Lieven: ''Grundriss des Laufes der Sterne''. p. 66. == Dating of Tepi-a Sopdet (Book of Nut System) == {| class="wikitable" style="width: 98%; border: 2px solid gray;" |- {{Ägyptologie TblHighlight}} |colspan="7" align=center style="background:#FFE39B"|'''Dating of Tepi-a Sopdet in Memphis''' |- {{Ägyptologie TblHighlight}} !Year ![[Culmination (astronomy)|Acronychal
Culmination]] ![[Acronychal|Acronychal
Setting]] ![[Heliacal rising|Heliacal
Rising]]The altitude values refer to the end of the respective night hour. Heliacal rising occurs before sunrise, acronychal setting, and acronychal culmination after sunset. !Remarks |- bgcolor="#fffaf0" | style="text-align:center" | 1873 BCE | style="text-align:center" | {{ill|Achet#Dating|lt=26th Achet II|de|26. Achet II|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Achet#Dating|qq=}}
January 11–12
Altitude 30.3°Actual culmination: 36.5°, approximately 2.75 hours after sunset. | style="text-align:center" | {{ill|Peret#Dating|lt=26th Peret I|de|26. Peret I|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Peret#Dating|qq=}}
April 11–12
Altitude 10.4°The observation of the setting was only possible towards the end of the decade. | style="text-align:center" | {{ill|Peret#Dating|lt=6th Peret IV|de|6. Peret IV|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Peret#Dating|qq=}}
June 20–21
Altitude 2.3°On the 10th decade day, the altitude was 9.3°. A heliacal observation during the decade was therefore not possible. | style="text-align:center" | [[:en:Senusret III|Senusret III]] |- bgcolor="#f5f5f5" | style="text-align:center" | 1541 BCE | style="text-align:center" | 19th Peret I
January 14–15 | style="text-align:center" | 19th Peret IV

April 15–16 | style="text-align:center" | 18th Shemu III
June 24–25 | style="text-align:center" | {{ill|Star clock|lt=Star clock|lt=Ramesside star clocks|de|Ramessidische Sternuhren|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ramesside star clocks|qq=}} |- |} == Literature == * [[:en:Christian Leitz|Christian Leitz]]: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren''. Peeters, Leuven 1995, ISBN 90-6831-669-9, p. 95. * {{ill|Alexandra von Lieven|fr|Alexandra von Lieven|de|Alexandra von Lieven|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Alexandra von Lieven|qq=}}: ''Grundriss des Laufes der Sterne – Das sogenannte Nutbuch''. The Carsten Niebuhr Institute of Ancient Eastern Studies (et al.), Copenhagen 2007, ISBN 978-87-635-0406-5, pp. 62 and 398. == References == [[Category:completion tier 1]] {{translated page|de|Tepi-a Sopdet|version=210539685|comment=Imported full de history}} {{draft categories| [[sn:Tepi-a-Sopdet]][[Category:Ancient Egyptian Decan Stars]] [[Category:WikiProject Ancient Egypt]][[sn:Tepi-a-Sopdet]][[Category:WikiProject Ancient Egypt]] [[Category:WikiProject Ancient Egypt]] [[sn:Tepi-a-Sopdet]] }} [[sn:Tepi-a-Sopdet]] [[de:Tepi-a Sopdet]] [[Category:Ancient Egyptian Decan Stars]]

revision.comment: bumping these so I can see the more important ones that have not been recently edited
revision.text:
{{Infobox hieroglyphs |NAME = D1:D36 M44-X1 N14-N33 |NAME-TRANSKRIPTION = Tepi-a Sopdet
''Tpj-ˁ Spd.t''
''Forerunner of Sopdet'' |BILD1 = Position Beta Cma.png |BILD1-BREITE = 250px |BILD1-BESCHREIBUNG = Constellation “Sopdet” ([[:en:Canis Major|Canis Major]]) }} '''Tepi-a Sopdet''' is the designation of an [[:en:Ancient Egypt|Ancient Egypt]] [[:en:Decan|Decan]], which comprised only one {{ill|Baktiu|1=Baktiu|2=de|3=Baktiu|de|Baktiu|1=Baktiu|2=de|3=Baktiu|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Baktiu|qq=}} (decan star). == Background == This decan corresponds to [[:en:Beta Canis Majoris|Murzim]], the forerunner of [[:en:Sirius|Sirius]]. The traditional [[:en:Arabic|Arabic language]] name, "Harbinger," likely refers to its position, as it rises before Sirius and heralds the brightest star in the night sky. However, this role does not apply to [[:en:Heliacal rising|Heliacal rising]] because Murzim is significantly dimmer than Sirius and requires a {{ill|Altitude|de|Sehungsbogen|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Altitude|qq=}} of at least 13° to become heliacally visible, or approximately 55 minutes before the sun rises. Furthermore, Murzim requires a minimum horizon altitude of 1.7° due to atmospheric refraction. The same conditions apply to {{ill|Acronychal setting|de|Akronychisch|1=Acronychic|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Acronychal setting|13=User:Immanuelle/Acronychic|qq=}}. The entries in the [[:en:Book of Nut|Book of Nut]] regarding decan positions can therefore only refer to the acronychal [[:en:Culmination|Culmination (astronomy)]], as only in this context could Murzim Rank before Sirius in the decan lists. During the era of the {{ill|Diagonal star clock|de|Diagonalsternuhren|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Diagonal star clock|qq=}}, Murzim reached a culmination altitude of approximately 36.6° in Ancient Egypt. Sirius itself was part of the Ancient Egyptian constellation {{ill|Sopdet (constellation)|de|Sopdet (Sternbild)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sopdet (constellation)|qq=}}. {{ill|Alexandra von Lieven|fr|Alexandra von Lieven|de|Alexandra von Lieven|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Alexandra von Lieven|qq=}} notes the decan star "Tepi-a Sopdet" mentioned in the [[:en:Book of Nut|Book of Nut]], which in the seventh year of [[:en:Senusret III|Senusret III]] was listed as the 34th decan with the [[:en:Heliacal rising|Heliacal rising]] assigned to the {{ill|Peret#Dating|lt=6th Peret IV|de|6. Peret IV|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Peret#Dating|qq=}} (approximately June 15/21); the [[:en:Culmination|Culmination (astronomy)]] for the {{ill|Achet#Dating|lt=26th Achet II|de|26. Achet II|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Achet#Dating|qq=}}, and the acronychal setting for the 26th Peret I. The recorded period of invisibility lasted the canonical 70 days.Alexandra von Lieven: ''Grundriss des Laufes der Sterne''. p. 66. == Dating of Tepi-a Sopdet (Book of Nut System) == {| class="wikitable" style="width: 98%; border: 2px solid gray;" |- {{Ägyptologie TblHighlight}} |colspan="7" align=center style="background:#FFE39B"|'''Dating of Tepi-a Sopdet in Memphis''' |- {{Ägyptologie TblHighlight}} !Year ![[Culmination (astronomy)|Acronychal
Culmination]] ![[Acronychal|Acronychal
Setting]] ![[Heliacal rising|Heliacal
Rising]]The altitude values refer to the end of the respective night hour. Heliacal rising occurs before sunrise, acronychal setting, and acronychal culmination after sunset. !Remarks |- bgcolor="#fffaf0" | style="text-align:center" | 1873 BCE | style="text-align:center" | {{ill|Achet#Dating|lt=26th Achet II|de|26. Achet II|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Achet#Dating|qq=}}
January 11–12
Altitude 30.3°Actual culmination: 36.5°, approximately 2.75 hours after sunset. | style="text-align:center" | {{ill|Peret#Dating|lt=26th Peret I|de|26. Peret I|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Peret#Dating|qq=}}
April 11–12
Altitude 10.4°The observation of the setting was only possible towards the end of the decade. | style="text-align:center" | {{ill|Peret#Dating|lt=6th Peret IV|de|6. Peret IV|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Peret#Dating|qq=}}
June 20–21
Altitude 2.3°On the 10th decade day, the altitude was 9.3°. A heliacal observation during the decade was therefore not possible. | style="text-align:center" | [[:en:Senusret III|Senusret III]] |- bgcolor="#f5f5f5" | style="text-align:center" | 1541 BCE | style="text-align:center" | 19th Peret I
January 14–15 | style="text-align:center" | 19th Peret IV
April 14–15 | style="text-align:center" | {{ill|Season of the Harvest|lt=Shemu#Dating|lt=29th Shemu II|de|29. Schemu II|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Season of the Harvest|qq=}}
June 23–24 | style="text-align:center" | {{ill|Ebers calendar|de|Ebers-Kalender|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ebers calendar|qq=}} |- bgcolor="#fffaf0" | style="text-align:center" | 1463 BCE | style="text-align:center" | 8th Peret II
January 15–16 | style="text-align:center" | 8th Shemu I
April 15–16 | style="text-align:center" | 18th Shemu III
June 24–25 | style="text-align:center" | {{ill|Star clock|lt=Star clock|lt=Ramesside star clocks|de|Ramessidische Sternuhren|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ramesside star clocks|qq=}} |- |} == Literature == * [[:en:Christian Leitz|Christian Leitz]]: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren''. Peeters, Leuven 1995, ISBN 90-6831-669-9, p. 95. * {{ill|Alexandra von Lieven|fr|Alexandra von Lieven|de|Alexandra von Lieven|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Alexandra von Lieven|qq=}}: ''Grundriss des Laufes der Sterne – Das sogenannte Nutbuch''. The Carsten Niebuhr Institute of Ancient Eastern Studies (et al.), Copenhagen 2007, ISBN 978-87-635-0406-5, pp. 62 and 398. == References == [[Category:completion tier 1]] {{translated page|de|Tepi-a Sopdet|version=210539685|comment=Imported full de history}} {{draft categories| [[sn:Tepi-a-Sopdet]][[Category:Ancient Egyptian Decan Stars]] [[Category:WikiProject Ancient Egypt]][[sn:Tepi-a-Sopdet]][[Category:WikiProject Ancient Egypt]] [[Category:WikiProject Ancient Egypt]] [[sn:Tepi-a-Sopdet]] }} [[sn:Tepi-a-Sopdet]] [[de:Tepi-a Sopdet]] [[Category:Ancient Egyptian Decan Stars]] [[Category:qq]]

The entries in the [[:en:Book of Nut|Book of Nut]] regarding decan positions can therefore only refer to the acronychal [[:en:Culmination|Culmination (astronomy)]], as only in this context could Murzim Rank before Sirius in the decan lists. During the era of the {{ill|Diagonal star clock|de|Diagonalsternuhren|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Diagonal star clock|qq=}}, Murzim reached a culmination altitude of approximately 36.6° in Ancient Egypt. Sirius itself was part of the Ancient Egyptian constellation {{ill|Sopdet (constellation)|de|Sopdet (Sternbild)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sopdet (constellation)|qq=}}. {{ill|Alexandra von Lieven|fr|Alexandra von Lieven|de|Alexandra von Lieven|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Alexandra von Lieven|qq=}} notes the decan star "Tepi-a Sopdet" mentioned in the [[:en:Book of Nut|Book of Nut]], which in the seventh year of [[:en:Senusret III|Senusret III]] was listed as the 34th decan with the [[:en:Heliacal rising|Heliacal rising]] assigned to the {{ill|Peret#Dating|lt=6th Peret IV|de|6. Peret IV|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Peret#Dating|qq=}} (approximately June 15/21); the [[:en:Culmination|Culmination (astronomy)]] for the {{ill|Achet#Dating|lt=26th Achet II|de|26. Achet II|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Achet#Dating|qq=}}, and the acronychal setting for the 26th Peret I. The recorded period of invisibility lasted the canonical 70 days.Alexandra von Lieven: ''Grundriss des Laufes der Sterne''. p. 66. == Dating of Tepi-a Sopdet (Book of Nut System) == {| class="wikitable" style="width: 98%; border: 2px solid gray;" |- {{Ägyptologie TblHighlight}} |colspan="7" align=center style="background:#FFE39B"|'''Dating of Tepi-a Sopdet in Memphis''' |- {{Ägyptologie TblHighlight}} !Year ![[Culmination (astronomy)|Acronychal
Culmination]] ![[Acronychal|Acronychal
Setting]] ![[Heliacal rising|Heliacal
Rising]]The altitude values refer to the end of the respective night hour. Heliacal rising occurs before sunrise, acronychal setting, and acronychal culmination after sunset. !Remarks |- bgcolor="#fffaf0" | style="text-align:center" | 1873 BCE | style="text-align:center" | {{ill|Achet#Dating|lt=26th Achet II|de|26. Achet II|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Achet#Dating|qq=}}
January 11–12
Altitude 30.3°Actual culmination: 36.5°, approximately 2.75 hours after sunset. | style="text-align:center" | {{ill|Peret#Dating|lt=26th Peret I|de|26. Peret I|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Peret#Dating|qq=}}
April 11–12
Altitude 10.4°The observation of the setting was only possible towards the end of the decade. | style="text-align:center" | {{ill|Peret#Dating|lt=6th Peret IV|de|6. Peret IV|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Peret#Dating|qq=}}
June 20–21
Altitude 2.3°On the 10th decade day, the altitude was 9.3°. A heliacal observation during the decade was therefore not possible. | style="text-align:center" | [[:en:Senusret III|Senusret III]] |- bgcolor="#f5f5f5" | style="text-align:center" | 1541 BCE | style="text-align:center" | 19th Peret I
January 14–15 | style="text-align:center" | 19th Peret IV
April 14–15 | style="text-align:center" | {{ill|Season of the Harvest|lt=Shemu#Dating|lt=29th Shemu II|de|29. Schemu II|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Season of the Harvest|qq=}}
June 23–24 | style="text-align:center" | {{ill|Ebers calendar|de|Ebers-Kalender|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ebers calendar|qq=}} |- bgcolor="#fffaf0" | style="text-align:center" | 1463 BCE | style="text-align:center" | 8th Peret II
January 15–16 | style="text-align:center" | 8th Shemu I
April 15–16 | style="text-align:center" | 18th Shemu III
June 24–25 | style="text-align:center" | {{ill|Star clock|lt=Star clock|lt=Ramesside star clocks|de|Ramessidische Sternuhren|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ramesside star clocks|qq=}} |- |} == Literature == * [[:en:Christian Leitz|Christian Leitz]]: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren''. Peeters, Leuven 1995, ISBN 90-6831-669-9, p. 95. * {{ill|Alexandra von Lieven|fr|Alexandra von Lieven|de|Alexandra von Lieven|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Alexandra von Lieven|qq=}}: ''Grundriss des Laufes der Sterne – Das sogenannte Nutbuch''. The Carsten Niebuhr Institute of Ancient Eastern Studies (et al.), Copenhagen 2007, ISBN 978-87-635-0406-5, pp. 62 and 398. == References == [[Category:completion tier 1]] {{translated page|de|Tepi-a Sopdet|version=210539685|comment=Imported full de history}} {{draft categories| [[sn:Tepi-a-Sopdet]][[Category:Ancient Egyptian Decan Stars]] [[Category:WikiProject Ancient Egypt]][[sn:Tepi-a-Sopdet]][[Category:WikiProject Ancient Egypt]] [[Category:WikiProject Ancient Egypt]] [[sn:Tepi-a-Sopdet]] }} [[sn:Tepi-a-Sopdet]] [[de:Tepi-a Sopdet]] [[Category:Ancient Egyptian Decan Stars]] [[sn:Tepi-a-Sopdet]] [[Category:Missing wikidata]]
